text: Forecourt traffic lanes and pedestrian routes are clearly marked and unobstructed.
hazardStatement: Vehicle-pedestrian conflict due to poor route definition.
consequenceStatement: Collision, injury, and disruption to station operations.
recommendation: Repaint lane markings and clear obstructions immediately.
guidanceText: Observe flow at peak periods and verify barriers remain in place.

text: Speed control signs and crossing controls are in place at all forecourt entry and exit points.
hazardStatement: Uncontrolled vehicle speed in mixed-use pedestrian area.
consequenceStatement: Severe injury or fatality involving customers and staff.
recommendation: Install temporary speed controls and enforce traffic marshal support.
guidanceText: Check both permanent signs and temporary controls during busy periods.

text: Spill kits are complete, visible, and immediately accessible on the forecourt.
hazardStatement: Delayed spill response due to unavailable kit materials.
consequenceStatement: Fuel release, environmental contamination, and fire escalation.
recommendation: Replenish spill kit and confirm placement at designated points.
guidanceText: Verify absorbents, PPE, booms, and disposal bags are all available.

text: Housekeeping standards are maintained with no trip hazards or unmanaged waste.
hazardStatement: Slip and trip exposure from poor housekeeping.
consequenceStatement: Minor to moderate injuries and customer complaints.
recommendation: Implement immediate clean-up and assign daily housekeeping checks.
guidanceText: Inspect drains, pump islands, walkways, and waste bins.

text: Emergency stop systems at dispenser islands are visible, accessible, and functional.
hazardStatement: Unable to isolate fuel flow during an emergency event.
consequenceStatement: Escalation to fire, explosion, and severe injuries.
recommendation: Test E-stop immediately and quarantine dispenser until restored.
guidanceText: Confirm labels, access, and test records are current.

text: Dispenser hoses, nozzles, and breakaway couplings are in sound condition.
hazardStatement: Fuel leakage from damaged transfer equipment.
consequenceStatement: Fire ignition, product loss, and exposure injuries.
recommendation: Isolate damaged nozzle/hoses and replace through approved maintenance.
guidanceText: Inspect physical wear, leaks, and coupling response.

text: Required fire extinguishers at dispensing points are in date and correctly mounted.
hazardStatement: Delayed fire suppression at ignition source.
consequenceStatement: Rapid fire spread and facility shutdown.
recommendation: Replace expired units and verify monthly inspection tagging.
guidanceText: Confirm type, pressure gauge, access, and inspection tags.

text: Bonding and static control signage is visible where fuel transfer occurs.
hazardStatement: Static ignition risk from inadequate transfer controls.
consequenceStatement: Localized flash fire and personnel injury.
recommendation: Restore signage and brief operators on static control steps.
guidanceText: Check signs at tanker points and pump islands.

text: Tank farm offloading procedure is posted and followed during product receipt.
hazardStatement: Unsafe offloading due to procedural non-adherence.
consequenceStatement: Overfill, spill, ignition, and major operational loss.
recommendation: Re-brief team and enforce checklist sign-off per delivery.
guidanceText: Observe a live or recent offloading operation where possible.

text: Overfill prevention and level alarm controls are tested within required intervals.
hazardStatement: Tank overfill due to failed instrumentation or alarms.
consequenceStatement: Major spill, fire escalation, and regulatory breach.
recommendation: Perform immediate functional test and lock out affected line until confirmed.
guidanceText: Review inspection certificates and live alarm indicators.

text: Bund walls and secondary containment are intact with no visible compromise.
hazardStatement: Loss of containment from failed bund infrastructure.
consequenceStatement: Widespread environmental impact and cleanup cost.
recommendation: Repair containment defects and verify integrity testing schedule.
guidanceText: Inspect wall cracks, floor integrity, and drain valves.

text: Drain isolation points in tank farm are secured and controlled.
hazardStatement: Uncontrolled discharge pathway during spill event.
consequenceStatement: Contamination of stormwater and potential fines.
recommendation: Install lock control and include drains in daily checks.
guidanceText: Verify drain map and valve status physically.

text: Vehicle grounding and wheel chocking controls are used during tanker transfer.
hazardStatement: Vehicle movement or static ignition during transfer.
consequenceStatement: Transfer-line failure, fire, and injury.
recommendation: Enforce grounding and chocking checklist before unloading.
guidanceText: Observe grounding cable and chock placement readiness.

text: LPG leak detection and gas monitoring checks are completed and recorded.
hazardStatement: Undetected LPG release in process area.
consequenceStatement: Flash fire or explosion with severe injury potential.
recommendation: Calibrate monitors and restore documented shift checks.
guidanceText: Review detector calibration tag and alarm test log.

text: LPG cylinders are segregated by status and stacked within safe limits.
hazardStatement: Cylinder instability and incompatible storage conditions.
consequenceStatement: Cylinder damage, release, and fire escalation.
recommendation: Re-stack and label cylinders by full/empty/defective status.
guidanceText: Confirm segregation lines and maximum stack heights.

text: Ignition source control (no smoking, spark control, bonding) is enforced in LPG zones.
hazardStatement: Ignition in flammable LPG atmosphere.
consequenceStatement: Explosion, fatalities, and major asset damage.
recommendation: Implement exclusion controls and immediate supervisor enforcement.
guidanceText: Verify signage, permits, and hot-work controls.

text: Emergency shower/eyewash and fire response points are accessible in LPG handling areas.
hazardStatement: Delayed first response for personnel exposure.
consequenceStatement: Increased injury severity and recovery time.
recommendation: Restore access and include checks in shift startup inspection.
guidanceText: Confirm path clearance and test accessibility.

text: LPG transfer hoses and couplings are inspected and within certification period.
hazardStatement: LPG leak from degraded hose/coupling.
consequenceStatement: Jet fire, burns, and emergency shutdown.
recommendation: Remove out-of-date hoses and replace with certified assemblies.
guidanceText: Check serial labels, expiry dates, and visible defects.

text: Required permits, certificates, and statutory licenses are valid and displayed.
hazardStatement: Regulatory non-compliance from expired approvals.
consequenceStatement: Sanctions, legal exposure, and operational disruption.
recommendation: Update license tracker and escalate pending renewals.
guidanceText: Review expiry dates against compliance schedule.

text: Mandatory HSSEQ and emergency training records are current for assigned staff.
hazardStatement: Inadequate response capability due to training gaps.
consequenceStatement: Unsafe decisions and delayed emergency response.
recommendation: Issue refresher plan and track completions monthly.
guidanceText: Sample records for operators, supervisors, and fire teams.

text: Current SDS sheets and hazardous material registers are available and accessible.
hazardStatement: Incorrect handling due to missing hazard information.
consequenceStatement: Chemical exposure and non-compliant storage practices.
recommendation: Update SDS index and post access instructions for all shifts.
guidanceText: Check digital and printed availability at point of use.

text: First aid kits and welfare facilities are stocked and fit for use.
hazardStatement: Delayed first aid treatment due to inadequate welfare readiness.
consequenceStatement: Worsened injury outcomes and workforce dissatisfaction.
recommendation: Replenish kits and assign monthly welfare checks.
guidanceText: Confirm contents against standard checklist and expiry dates.

text: Incident and hazard communication board is updated with current actions and learnings.
hazardStatement: Reduced awareness of current HSSEQ risks and controls.
consequenceStatement: Repeat issues due to poor communication.
recommendation: Update board weekly and confirm team briefing sign-off.
guidanceText: Board should show open critical items and due actions.

text: Electrical panels are labeled, secured, and protected from unauthorized access.
hazardStatement: Electrical shock or arc event from uncontrolled access.
consequenceStatement: Serious injury, fire initiation, or prolonged outage.
recommendation: Secure panel access and restore lockout controls immediately.
guidanceText: Verify lock status, labels, and permit signage.

text: No combustible materials are stored in the electrical room.
hazardStatement: Fire spread from combustible materials near energized equipment.
consequenceStatement: Electrical fire, downtime, and injury risk.
recommendation: Clear stored items and enforce electrical-room housekeeping standard.
guidanceText: Check cabinet tops, corners, and temporary storage items.

text: Earthing, insulation, and periodic electrical test certificates are valid.
hazardStatement: Latent electrical faults without validated testing.
consequenceStatement: Shock, arc flash, and equipment damage.
recommendation: Arrange certified testing and update compliance certificates.
guidanceText: Cross-check report dates against required intervals.

text: Electrical room access control signage and emergency contact details are posted.
hazardStatement: Unauthorized access during abnormal electrical conditions.
consequenceStatement: Potential injury and delayed emergency response.
recommendation: Post updated signage and contact points at room entrance.
guidanceText: Ensure emergency number and lock instructions are visible.

text: Waste oil and lubricant handling controls prevent leaks and uncontrolled discharge.
hazardStatement: Hydrocarbon contamination from poor lubricant handling.
consequenceStatement: Environmental release and cleanup cost.
recommendation: Install secondary containment and enforce decanting procedure.
guidanceText: Inspect drain points, storage drums, and transfer methods.

text: Lifting equipment and jacks are certified and inspected before use.
hazardStatement: Equipment failure during lifting operation.
consequenceStatement: Crush injury and severe asset damage.
recommendation: Withdraw untested equipment and schedule certification.
guidanceText: Validate inspection stickers and condition of lifting tools.

text: Workshop and tyre staff use required PPE consistently.
hazardStatement: Direct exposure to mechanical and chemical hazards.
consequenceStatement: Injury, lost-time incidents, and non-compliance.
recommendation: Reinforce PPE checks and supervisor enforcement.
guidanceText: Observe active tasks and check PPE stock availability.

text: Tyre inflation and pressure testing controls are safe and compliant.
hazardStatement: Tyre burst during inflation due to poor control.
consequenceStatement: Impact injuries and equipment damage.
recommendation: Use inflation cage and calibrated gauges consistently.
guidanceText: Check hose condition, gauge calibration, and shielding.

text: Oil-water separator at wash bay is operational and serviced as scheduled.
hazardStatement: Contaminated discharge from failed treatment system.
consequenceStatement: Environmental breach and possible penalties.
recommendation: Stop discharge, service separator, and validate sample results.
guidanceText: Review maintenance log and inspect separators physically.

text: Wash bay drainage is free-flowing, clearly channeled, and free from standing contamination.
hazardStatement: Slip hazards and uncontrolled runoff in service area.
consequenceStatement: Minor injuries and local contamination.
recommendation: Clear channels and enforce daily wash bay cleanup.
guidanceText: Check drains at start and end of operational shift.

text: Electrical fittings in wash bay are weatherproof and protected from water ingress.
hazardStatement: Electrical shorting from water exposure.
consequenceStatement: Shock hazard and fire risk in wet environment.
recommendation: Repair damaged fittings and verify IP-rated enclosures.
guidanceText: Inspect sockets, conduit seals, and isolator condition.

text: Warehouse/depot stacking, aisle clearance, and storage zoning are compliant.
hazardStatement: Falling objects and obstructed evacuation routes.
consequenceStatement: Injury and delayed emergency response.
recommendation: Reorganize storage and repaint zone markings.
guidanceText: Verify aisle width and max stack labels in each bay.

text: Fire hydrants, extinguishers, and hose reels are accessible throughout warehouse/depot zones.
hazardStatement: Inadequate firefighting reach in high-load storage area.
consequenceStatement: Rapid fire spread and major stock loss.
recommendation: Clear access routes and conduct weekly firefighting readiness checks.
guidanceText: Verify that no pallets or drums block emergency equipment.

text: Loading and dispatch areas operate with documented traffic and pedestrian separation controls.
hazardStatement: Vehicle movement conflict in loading zones.
consequenceStatement: Serious injury, loading delay, and operational disruption.
recommendation: Deploy marshals and enforce one-way loading movement plan.
guidanceText: Review live loading cycle and route barricading.

text: Transfer lines, valves, and hose couplings in depot tank interfaces show no leaks or damage.
hazardStatement: Loss of containment from degraded line interfaces.
consequenceStatement: Major release and potential ignition event.
recommendation: Isolate affected line and issue urgent maintenance work order.
guidanceText: Inspect gasket condition, flange marks, and valve packings.

text: Depot spill response drills are conducted and corrective lessons are tracked.
hazardStatement: Poor spill response coordination during real events.
consequenceStatement: Longer incident duration and wider impact area.
recommendation: Schedule quarterly drills and assign closure for observed gaps.
guidanceText: Review drill records and unresolved post-drill actions.

text: Retail shop and restaurant emergency exits remain unobstructed and clearly signed.
hazardStatement: Blocked egress during customer-area emergency.
consequenceStatement: Delayed evacuation and crowd panic.
recommendation: Reinforce housekeeping around exits and place directional signs.
guidanceText: Inspect both front-of-house and back-of-house egress routes.

text: Are entrances and exits clear, accessible, and free from obstruction?
hazardStatement: Blocked entrances or exits can trap occupants during emergencies and cause congestion injuries.
consequenceStatement: Entrapment, delayed evacuation, crush injuries.
recommendation: Conduct daily walkthrough checks of all entrance and exit points and remove any obstructions immediately.
guidanceText: Check that all doors open freely and paths are clear of furniture, boxes, or equipment.

text: Are passageways free from trip hazards such as loose cables, stored items, or uneven surfaces?
hazardStatement: Loose cables, stored items, or uneven floors are leading causes of workplace trip incidents.
consequenceStatement: Sprains, fractures, and soft tissue injuries from falls.
recommendation: Use cable management solutions and maintain regular passageway inspections.
guidanceText: Pay particular attention to areas near workstations and photocopier areas where cables accumulate.

text: Are floors in good condition, clean, dry, and free from slip hazards?
hazardStatement: Wet, dirty, or damaged floors significantly increase slip and fall risk.
consequenceStatement: Slip injuries ranging from minor bruising to fractures.
recommendation: Implement a cleaning schedule and use wet floor signs during and after cleaning.
guidanceText: Check near entrances after rain, kitchen areas, and near sinks or water dispensers.

text: Are stairs and handrails in safe condition and well maintained?
hazardStatement: Defective stairs or missing handrails greatly increase the risk of falls from height.
consequenceStatement: Serious injuries including fractures and head injuries from stair falls.
recommendation: Inspect all staircases monthly and repair defects immediately.
guidanceText: Check each tread for wear, check handrails are secure on both sides, and ensure landings are unobstructed.

text: Are doors, corridors, and access routes wide enough for safe movement of people and office equipment?
hazardStatement: Narrow corridors increase the risk of collisions and impede emergency evacuation.
consequenceStatement: Collision injuries and delayed evacuation.
recommendation: Ensure minimum corridor widths are maintained and review layout when moving furniture or equipment.
guidanceText: Check compliance with minimum access width requirements, typically 1.2 m for main circulation routes.

text: Is the office layout arranged to allow safe movement between work areas, meeting rooms, storage areas, and exits?
hazardStatement: Poor layout forces staff to take unsafe routes or carry items through congested areas.
consequenceStatement: Musculoskeletal strain, collisions, and trip injuries.
recommendation: Review office layout periodically and ensure logical, direct routes to key areas.
guidanceText: Walk the route from each workstation to the nearest exit and note any awkward navigation.

text: Are frequently used areas easy to access without awkward reaching, squeezing, or unsafe movement?
hazardStatement: Difficult access to frequently used locations encourages unsafe postures and movement.
consequenceStatement: Musculoskeletal injuries and minor strain injuries.
recommendation: Reposition frequently accessed items and areas to reduce awkward access.
guidanceText: Observe how staff access printers, filing cabinets, and shared equipment.

text: Is lighting adequate in walkways, entrances, stairways, and shared access areas?
hazardStatement: Inadequate lighting obscures trip hazards and increases the risk of falls, especially on stairs.
consequenceStatement: Falls and collision injuries in poorly lit areas.
recommendation: Maintain lighting levels to recognised standards and replace failed luminaires promptly.
guidanceText: Check lux levels are adequate, particularly in transition zones between light and dark areas.

text: Are emergency exits clearly identified, illuminated where required, and immediately accessible?
hazardStatement: Unmarked or blocked emergency exits can cause fatal delays during evacuation.
consequenceStatement: Multiple fatalities or serious injuries in emergency situations.
recommendation: Test emergency exit signage and lighting monthly and remove all obstructions.
guidanceText: Check that exit signs are lit, clearly visible, and that no furniture or storage blocks exit doors.

text: Do exit routes lead safely to a suitable assembly point or safe external area?
hazardStatement: Exit routes that lead to hazardous areas or dead ends can trap evacuees.
consequenceStatement: Injuries or fatalities during emergency evacuation.
recommendation: Review and test all escape routes annually and after any layout changes.
guidanceText: Walk each exit route from end to end and confirm it reaches the designated assembly point.

text: Are fire doors, exit doors, and access control doors functioning properly and not improperly locked or blocked?
hazardStatement: Locked or defective fire and exit doors are a critical life safety failure.
consequenceStatement: Entrapment and fatalities during fire or emergency.
recommendation: Test all emergency doors monthly, ensure self-closing mechanisms work, and prohibit wedging open fire doors.
guidanceText: Check door closers, release mechanisms, and intumescent seals. Confirm no fire door is propped open.

text: Are accessibility provisions in place for people with disabilities, including access to entrances, routes, and key facilities?
hazardStatement: Inadequate accessibility provisions may exclude or endanger people with disabilities.
consequenceStatement: Injury risk and legal non-compliance.
recommendation: Audit facilities against accessibility standards and implement required provisions.
guidanceText: Check for ramps, wide doorways, accessible toilets, and clear signage for people with mobility or visual impairments.

text: Are ramps, lifts, and other access aids available, operational, and unobstructed where required?
hazardStatement: Non-functional access aids can strand or injure occupants with mobility impairments.
consequenceStatement: Falls, entrapment, and inability to evacuate safely.
recommendation: Carry out regular maintenance on lifts and ramps and report defects immediately.
guidanceText: Check lift service records, inspect ramp surfaces for damage, and confirm access aids are not obstructed.

text: Are signs for rooms, exits, restricted areas, and key access points clear and easy to understand?
hazardStatement: Missing or unclear signage leads to confusion, especially during emergencies.
consequenceStatement: Delayed response, misdirection during evacuation.
recommendation: Review all signage after layout changes and replace any damaged or faded signs.
guidanceText: Walk the facility from the perspective of a new visitor and assess whether signs provide clear direction.

text: Is storage arranged so that items do not obstruct access routes, doorways, fire equipment, or exits?
hazardStatement: Stored items blocking exits or fire equipment significantly compromise emergency response.
consequenceStatement: Delayed evacuation, inability to fight incipient fire.
recommendation: Enforce a strict no-storage policy within 1 m of fire equipment, exits, and access routes.
guidanceText: Check around fire extinguishers, break glass points, fire hose reels, and all emergency exit routes.

text: Can staff and visitors move safely through reception, common areas, and workspaces without congestion?
hazardStatement: Congested areas increase collision risk and impede emergency evacuation.
consequenceStatement: Minor collision injuries and evacuation delays.
recommendation: Review occupant density and reorganise furniture or workstations to reduce congestion.
guidanceText: Observe peak-time movement patterns in reception and common areas.

text: Are high-risk access points such as stairs, level changes, glass doors, or restricted areas adequately marked and controlled?
hazardStatement: Unmarked hazardous access points increase the likelihood of accidental injury.
consequenceStatement: Falls, lacerations, and crush injuries.
recommendation: Apply hazard markings, install visual warnings on glass doors, and use appropriate barriers at level changes.
guidanceText: Check for yellow step nosings, glass door manifestations, and safety barriers at raised platforms.

text: Is there safe access for cleaners, maintenance personnel, and contractors to the areas they need to reach?
hazardStatement: Unsafe access for maintenance or cleaning staff leads to improvised and dangerous work methods.
consequenceStatement: Falls, equipment damage, and unauthorised access to restricted areas.
recommendation: Provide safe, designated access routes for maintenance and cleaning activities.
guidanceText: Review contractor access arrangements and ensure appropriate supervision and permits are in place.

text: Is the overall layout suitable for the number of occupants and the activities carried out in the office?
hazardStatement: Overcrowded or poorly configured spaces increase stress, collision risk, and fire hazard.
consequenceStatement: Physical and psychological health impacts, evacuation difficulties.
recommendation: Review space allocation against occupancy standards and adjust layout or occupancy limits accordingly.
guidanceText: Calculate available floor space per person and compare against minimum requirements.

text: Work surfaces are set up at appropriate height for tasks undertaken
hazardStatement: Work surfaces at incorrect height cause awkward postures and musculoskeletal strain.
consequenceStatement: Musculoskeletal injury and discomfort affecting productivity.
recommendation: Adjust or replace work surfaces to appropriate heights; provide height-adjustable desks where feasible.
guidanceText: Observe whether workers can maintain neutral posture when using work surfaces.

text: Layout of work area is suitable for tasks and reduces bending, twisting and overreaching
hazardStatement: Poor work area layout requires awkward postures that increase musculoskeletal injury risk.
consequenceStatement: Musculoskeletal strain from repeated awkward postures.
recommendation: Reorganise work areas so that frequently used items and tools are within easy reach without awkward postures.
guidanceText: Assess whether personnel need to bend, twist, or overreach to perform routine tasks.

text: Heavy or frequently used items are stored at waist height
hazardStatement: Storing heavy items too high or low increases manual handling injury risk.
consequenceStatement: Back, shoulder, or neck injury from awkward manual handling.
recommendation: Relocate heavy and frequently accessed items to between knee and shoulder height in all storage areas.
guidanceText: Check storage heights for heavy or frequently used items at workstations and in storage areas.

text: Persons are protected from sharp objects
hazardStatement: Exposed sharp objects at workstations cause lacerations and puncture injuries.
consequenceStatement: Cuts, lacerations, or puncture wounds to personnel.
recommendation: Cover or remove sharp edges; store sharp items safely; provide appropriate PPE where exposure cannot be eliminated.
guidanceText: Inspect workstations and storage areas for exposed sharp edges, corners, or tools.

text: Free standing fittings are secure and stable
hazardStatement: Unstable free-standing fittings can tip and fall, causing injury.
consequenceStatement: Crush injury or impact injury from falling fittings.
recommendation: Secure free-standing shelving, screens, and equipment to walls or floors; check stability regularly.
guidanceText: Push or test free-standing items to check for stability; look for items at risk of tipping.

text: Serviceability of office equipment is appropriate
hazardStatement: Faulty or poorly maintained office equipment causes discomfort, errors, and potential injury.
consequenceStatement: Reduced productivity, equipment damage, or injury from defective equipment.
recommendation: Implement a routine equipment inspection and maintenance programme; report and repair faults promptly.
guidanceText: Check office equipment such as chairs, desks, and shared devices for obvious faults or damage.

text: Storage is designed to minimise manual handling problems (heavier and frequently used items between knee and shoulder height)
hazardStatement: Poorly designed storage forces awkward postures and increases manual handling risk.
consequenceStatement: Musculoskeletal injury from repeated awkward access to stored items.
recommendation: Review storage layouts; position heavy and frequently accessed items in the knee-to-shoulder height zone.
guidanceText: Inspect storage areas and check whether heavy items are stored at appropriate heights.

text: Ergonomic chairs with adjustable height, back tilt and lumbar support are provided for workstations
hazardStatement: Non-ergonomic or non-adjustable chairs cause poor posture and musculoskeletal injury for office workers.
consequenceStatement: Back, neck, and shoulder musculoskeletal disorders from sustained incorrect posture.
recommendation: Provide ergonomic chairs at all workstations; train personnel on correct adjustment.
guidanceText: Check chairs at workstations for height adjustment, lumbar support, and back tilt functionality.

text: Desktop space is sufficient
hazardStatement: Insufficient desktop space causes clutter, awkward postures, and increased error risk.
consequenceStatement: Musculoskeletal strain and reduced work quality due to cramped workstation.
recommendation: Ensure workstation desks provide sufficient clear space for the tasks performed; remove unnecessary items.
guidanceText: Observe workstations for clutter and check whether personnel have adequate clear working space.

text: Desktop setup allows the user to sit directly in front of the computer monitor without twisting or bending
hazardStatement: Monitors positioned to the side cause sustained neck rotation and musculoskeletal strain.
consequenceStatement: Neck and shoulder musculoskeletal disorders from sustained twisted posture.
recommendation: Position monitors directly in front of the user; adjust height so the top of the screen is at eye level.
guidanceText: Check monitor positioning at workstations; observe whether users need to twist or tilt their head to view the screen.

text: Computer monitors are positioned away from direct glare (sunlight, reflections, etc.)
hazardStatement: Glare on monitors causes visual discomfort, eye strain, and adoption of awkward postures to avoid reflection.
consequenceStatement: Eye strain, headaches, and musculoskeletal strain from posture adjustments to avoid glare.
recommendation: Reposition monitors perpendicular to windows; use blinds or screen filters to control glare.
guidanceText: Check monitor placement relative to windows and light sources; look for visible reflections on screens.

text: Computer screens are clear, legible and stable (no flickering)
hazardStatement: Flickering or unclear screens cause eye strain and headaches.
consequenceStatement: Eye fatigue, headaches, and reduced work quality.
recommendation: Replace or repair screens that flicker or have poor display quality; ensure resolution and refresh rate settings are correct.
guidanceText: Ask users whether screens flicker or are difficult to read; visually inspect screens during inspection.

text: Filing cabinets and shelves are secured to avoid tipping over
hazardStatement: Unsecured filing cabinets and shelves can tip when drawers are opened, causing crush injury.
consequenceStatement: Crush injury or impact injury from tipping furniture.
recommendation: Anchor tall filing cabinets and shelving units to walls; check stability of all tall furniture.
guidanceText: Check that filing cabinets and tall shelves are wall-anchored or otherwise secured against tipping.

text: Personnel understand ergonomic hazards in the workplace and how to protect themselves from musculoskeletal injuries
hazardStatement: Personnel unaware of ergonomic hazards cannot take steps to protect themselves from musculoskeletal injury.
consequenceStatement: Musculoskeletal disorders due to lack of hazard awareness.
recommendation: Provide ergonomic awareness training during induction and refresher training as required; include workstation setup guidance.
guidanceText: Ask personnel about ergonomic hazards in their work area; check training records for ergonomic awareness training.

text: There is no indication from personnel of musculoskeletal injuries as a result of work activities
hazardStatement: Unreported or unaddressed musculoskeletal symptoms indicate ongoing ergonomic risk.
consequenceStatement: Progressive musculoskeletal disorders that may become disabling if not addressed.
recommendation: Review incident and health records for musculoskeletal complaints; conduct ergonomic assessments where concerns are raised.
guidanceText: Ask supervisors and personnel whether musculoskeletal discomfort or injuries have been reported; check records.

text: Equipment and tools are in good working condition and properly maintained
hazardStatement: Faulty or poorly maintained tools and equipment increase injury risk and reduce work quality.
consequenceStatement: Injury or equipment failure during use.
recommendation: Implement routine equipment maintenance; train staff to inspect before use and report defects promptly.
guidanceText: Inspect tools and equipment at workstations for visible damage, wear, or malfunction.

text: Equipment and hand tools are appropriate for the worker and job task
hazardStatement: Ill-fitting or inappropriate tools force awkward grip and postures, increasing musculoskeletal risk.
consequenceStatement: Musculoskeletal injury and reduced work effectiveness.
recommendation: Select ergonomically designed tools appropriate for the task and the user; assess fit for workers of different sizes.
guidanceText: Observe tools being used; check whether they suit the task and user without requiring awkward grip or posture.

text: Work stations, surfaces and equipment are at an appropriate height for the type of work performed
hazardStatement: Incorrect working heights cause sustained awkward posture and musculoskeletal strain.
consequenceStatement: Musculoskeletal disorders from prolonged work at incorrect heights.
recommendation: Adjust or replace workstations and equipment to appropriate heights for the tasks and users; provide anti-fatigue matting where standing work is required.
guidanceText: Check working heights for seated and standing tasks; assess whether personnel maintain neutral posture.

text: Chairs are appropriate for the task and in good condition
hazardStatement: Worn or task-inappropriate chairs lead to poor posture and musculoskeletal injury.
consequenceStatement: Back, hip, and posture-related musculoskeletal disorders.
recommendation: Replace worn or broken chairs; ensure chair type matches the task; adjust to the user before use.
guidanceText: Inspect chairs for damage, missing castors, non-functioning adjustments, or worn padding.

text: No sustained or awkward posture is required during performance of work activities
hazardStatement: Sustained or awkward postures are a primary cause of musculoskeletal disorders.
consequenceStatement: Work-related musculoskeletal disorders leading to lost time and disability.
recommendation: Redesign tasks, workstations, or processes to eliminate sustained awkward postures; introduce task rotation.
guidanceText: Observe personnel performing work; identify whether any tasks require sustained bending, twisting, or overreaching.

text: Personnel understand and practice good lifting techniques
hazardStatement: Incorrect manual handling technique is a major cause of back and musculoskeletal injury.
consequenceStatement: Acute or cumulative back and musculoskeletal injury from manual handling.
recommendation: Provide manual handling training to all staff who lift; include correct technique, load limits, and use of aids.
guidanceText: Observe lifting activities; ask personnel to describe safe lifting technique; check training records.

text: Heavy objects are stored on shelves at approximately waist height
hazardStatement: Heavy objects stored at floor or overhead level require awkward lifting that increases injury risk.
consequenceStatement: Back and shoulder injury from lifting heavy items from awkward heights.
recommendation: Relocate heavy items to the waist-height storage zone; use mechanical lifting aids for items stored at other levels.
guidanceText: Check storage areas for heavy items stored at floor level or above shoulder height.

text: Materials stored above shoulder height are secured so that they do not fall
hazardStatement: Unsecured items stored above shoulder height can fall and cause head or body injury.
consequenceStatement: Impact injury from falling stored materials.
recommendation: Secure items stored above shoulder height with lips, straps, or other retention; avoid storing heavy items overhead.
guidanceText: Inspect overhead storage areas for unsecured or unstable items that could fall.

text: Materials being handled are of a manageable size and weight
hazardStatement: Handling items that are too heavy or bulky significantly increases the risk of musculoskeletal injury.
consequenceStatement: Acute or cumulative musculoskeletal injury from handling excessive loads.
recommendation: Assess manual handling tasks; split loads, provide mechanical aids, or use team lifts where loads exceed safe limits.
guidanceText: Observe manual handling tasks; check whether loads are within safe weight and size limits for the number of handlers.

text: Frequently used items are placed close at hand
hazardStatement: Reaching repeatedly for items not within comfortable reach causes musculoskeletal strain.
consequenceStatement: Shoulder, neck, and upper limb musculoskeletal disorders from repeated reaching.
recommendation: Arrange workstations so that frequently used items are within the primary reach zone without stretching.
guidanceText: Observe workstation layouts; check whether frequently used items are accessible without reaching or twisting.

text: Is temperature in the work area suitable for the work being carried out?
hazardStatement: Uncomfortable temperatures reduce concentration and can cause heat stress or cold-related illness.
consequenceStatement: Discomfort, reduced productivity, or occupational health complaints.
recommendation: Maintain workspace temperatures between 18–26°C; provide heating/cooling controls where possible.
guidanceText: Check thermostat settings and whether staff have reported temperature complaints.

text: Is ventilation adequate to provide fresh air and maintain comfort?
hazardStatement: Poor ventilation leads to CO₂ build-up, fatigue, and spread of airborne contaminants.
consequenceStatement: Headaches, fatigue, increased respiratory illness, and reduced cognitive performance.
recommendation: Ensure mechanical or natural ventilation provides adequate fresh air changes per hour.
guidanceText: Verify that windows can open or HVAC is operational and supplying fresh air.

text: Is indoor air quality acceptable, with no excessive dust, fumes, smoke, or unpleasant odours?
hazardStatement: Poor indoor air quality can cause respiratory irritation, allergic reactions, and long-term health effects.
consequenceStatement: Occupational illness, staff complaints, and potential regulatory breaches.
recommendation: Identify and eliminate sources of contamination; increase ventilation rates if required.
guidanceText: Look for visible dust, chemical odours, or reports of eye/throat irritation from staff.

text: Is lighting adequate and appropriate for the tasks being performed?
hazardStatement: Inadequate lighting causes eye strain, headaches, and increases the risk of errors and accidents.
consequenceStatement: Reduced accuracy, eye fatigue, and potential for accidents in poorly lit areas.
recommendation: Ensure lux levels meet task requirements; replace failed bulbs and add task lighting where needed.
guidanceText: Check that workstations, walkways, and storage areas are adequately lit.

text: Are glare, reflections, or excessive brightness adequately controlled?
hazardStatement: Glare and reflections cause visual discomfort and can impair ability to see screens or work safely.
consequenceStatement: Eye strain, headaches, and reduced work quality.
recommendation: Use diffusers, blinds, or screen filters to control glare; reposition monitors away from direct light sources.
guidanceText: Observe workstations at different times of day; ask staff about glare issues.

text: Is noise at an acceptable level and controlled so it does not interfere with work, communication, or wellbeing?
hazardStatement: Excessive noise causes distraction, stress, communication difficulties, and potential hearing damage.
consequenceStatement: Errors, stress-related illness, reduced productivity, and hearing damage with prolonged exposure.
recommendation: Identify noise sources; implement acoustic controls, zoning, or hearing protection where levels are unacceptable.
guidanceText: Check whether staff complain about noise; take readings if levels appear high.

text: Are heating, cooling, and ventilation systems functioning properly?
hazardStatement: Faulty HVAC systems result in poor air quality, temperature extremes, and potential fire or health hazards.
consequenceStatement: Discomfort, health complaints, potential equipment damage, and regulatory non-compliance.
recommendation: Schedule regular HVAC maintenance and inspect filters, ducts, and controls for proper operation.
guidanceText: Check service records and observe whether the system is operating as expected.

text: Is the overall workplace environment comfortable and suitable for safe, effective work?
hazardStatement: An uncomfortable or unsuitable environment reduces productivity and increases risk of error and injury.
consequenceStatement: Reduced effectiveness, higher error rates, and increased stress or fatigue.
recommendation: Conduct regular workplace comfort surveys and address staff concerns promptly.
guidanceText: Ask staff about comfort issues and observe conditions during the inspection.

text: Are blinds, curtains, or other shading controls available and functioning where needed?
hazardStatement: Uncontrolled sunlight causes glare, heat build-up, and discomfort at workstations.
consequenceStatement: Eye strain, overheating, and reduced screen visibility.
recommendation: Inspect blinds and shading for damage or missing components; replace as needed.
guidanceText: Check that window shading is available and accessible in areas with direct sunlight.

text: Is there no evidence of dampness, leaks, or mould affecting the work environment?
hazardStatement: Dampness and mould cause respiratory illness, allergic reactions, and structural damage.
consequenceStatement: Occupational illness, staff complaints, building damage, and regulatory non-compliance.
recommendation: Identify and fix the source of moisture; remove mould using appropriate methods and PPE; improve ventilation.
guidanceText: Look for water stains, peeling paint, musty odours, or visible mould on walls and ceilings.

text: Is air movement adequate to prevent stuffiness or stagnant conditions?
hazardStatement: Stagnant air leads to discomfort, fatigue, and build-up of airborne contaminants.
consequenceStatement: Reduced alertness, headaches, and increased spread of airborne illness.
recommendation: Ensure air circulation is adequate; use fans or adjust HVAC settings to improve air movement.
guidanceText: Check whether areas feel stuffy or still; verify that air vents are unobstructed.

text: Are work areas protected from excessive external noise or other environmental disturbance?
hazardStatement: External noise and disturbances reduce concentration and increase stress.
consequenceStatement: Errors, fatigue, stress-related illness, and reduced communication effectiveness.
recommendation: Use acoustic insulation, double glazing, or relocation of workstations away from noise sources.
guidanceText: Identify whether external noise (traffic, machinery) is affecting staff; consider acoustic surveys if high.

text: Is there a documented and current emergency response plan for the facility?
hazardStatement: Without a documented emergency response plan, personnel may not respond correctly during an emergency.
consequenceStatement: Delayed evacuation, injury, or fatality due to lack of clear emergency guidance.
recommendation: Develop, document, and review the emergency response plan at least annually; ensure it is accessible to all staff.
guidanceText: Check that the plan is dated, reviewed, and covers evacuation procedures, contacts, and roles.

text: Are fire and evacuation procedures clearly communicated to staff, contractors, and visitors?
hazardStatement: Staff unaware of procedures may not evacuate correctly or assist others during an emergency.
consequenceStatement: Delayed or disorderly evacuation, potential injury.
recommendation: Include emergency procedures in induction training and post them prominently throughout the facility.
guidanceText: Ask a random staff member to describe the evacuation procedure; check induction records.

text: Are emergency contact details, alarm instructions, and key response information clearly displayed?
hazardStatement: Lack of visible emergency information delays response when seconds matter.
consequenceStatement: Delayed emergency response and potential harm.
recommendation: Post emergency contacts, alarm instructions, and first responder details on noticeboards, near exits, and in reception.
guidanceText: Check noticeboards and key areas for up-to-date emergency information displays.

text: Is a site evacuation plan in place, current, and available where required?
hazardStatement: Without a current evacuation plan, occupants may not know escape routes or assembly points.
consequenceStatement: Disorganised evacuation leading to injury or fatality.
recommendation: Post evacuation plans on each floor, near fire exits, and at reception; review whenever layout changes.
guidanceText: Verify plans are posted visibly, include current layout, and identify assembly points.

text: Are designated assembly points identified, accessible, and known to personnel?
hazardStatement: Unknown or inaccessible assembly points cause confusion and headcount failures during evacuation.
consequenceStatement: Delayed accountability, potential rescue delays, and additional risk to emergency responders.
recommendation: Clearly mark assembly points, include them on evacuation plans, and confirm staff awareness during induction and drills.
guidanceText: Check that assembly point signs are visible and staff can identify the location without hesitation.

text: Are fire wardens / marshals appointed, identifiable, and aware of their responsibilities where required?
hazardStatement: Without designated wardens, evacuation coordination and sweep of areas may not occur.
consequenceStatement: Incomplete evacuation, unaccounted personnel, and delayed emergency response.
recommendation: Appoint and train fire wardens; issue identifying vests or armbands; review appointments when personnel change.
guidanceText: Check appointment records and confirm wardens know their duties and coverage areas.

text: Have fire drills or emergency evacuation drills been carried out and recorded at appropriate intervals?
hazardStatement: Untested evacuations are slower, more chaotic, and more likely to result in injury.
consequenceStatement: Poor evacuation performance, potential injury, and regulatory non-compliance.
recommendation: Conduct and record at least one fire drill per year; review outcomes and correct deficiencies.
guidanceText: Check drill records for date, participation, outcomes, and any corrective actions raised.

text: Are fire extinguishers provided where required, appropriate to the risks present, and easily accessible?
hazardStatement: Missing or inappropriate extinguishers leave occupants unable to tackle incipient fires safely.
consequenceStatement: Fire escalation, potential fatalities, and significant property damage.
recommendation: Conduct a fire risk assessment to determine required extinguisher types and locations; ensure clear access and signage.
guidanceText: Verify extinguisher type (A, B, CO₂ etc.) matches risks in the area; check that they are unobstructed.

text: Are fire extinguishers inspected, serviced, and within valid maintenance dates?
hazardStatement: Unserviced extinguishers may fail when needed, rendering fire-fighting attempts ineffective.
consequenceStatement: Inability to control incipient fire, leading to fire escalation and injury.
recommendation: Service all extinguishers annually by a competent person; check tags and seals monthly; replace out-of-date units immediately.
guidanceText: Check service tags on extinguishers; confirm last service date and next due date are current.

text: Are fire alarm systems, manual call points, detectors, and warning devices present and in working order?
hazardStatement: Faulty or absent alarm systems mean fires may not be detected or reported in time to permit safe evacuation.
consequenceStatement: Delayed or failed evacuation, leading to fatalities and severe property loss.
recommendation: Test alarm systems regularly; ensure service records are maintained; replace faulty components immediately.
guidanceText: Check alarm test records; inspect call points and detectors for damage or obstruction.

text: Can alarms be clearly heard or otherwise effectively detected in offices, stores, and yard areas?
hazardStatement: Alarms that cannot be heard in all areas may leave occupants unaware of an emergency.
consequenceStatement: Delayed evacuation and potential injury to occupants in areas with poor alarm coverage.
recommendation: Test alarm audibility across all areas; add visual alarms, vibrating alerts, or additional sounders where needed.
guidanceText: Walk through all areas during alarm test to confirm audibility; check for visual alert devices where noise is high.

text: Are escape routes and emergency exits clearly marked, unobstructed, and immediately usable?
hazardStatement: Obstructed or unmarked exits prevent occupants from escaping safely during a fire.
consequenceStatement: Trapped occupants, fatalities, and regulatory non-compliance.
recommendation: Inspect escape routes daily; ensure all exits are signed, lit, and free from obstruction at all times.
guidanceText: Walk all escape routes; check for obstructions, broken signs, and missing emergency lighting.

text: Are emergency exits easy to open and not locked, blocked, or otherwise restricted during occupancy?
hazardStatement: Locked or blocked exits trap occupants during a fire emergency.
consequenceStatement: Fatalities due to inability to escape, and severe legal liability.
recommendation: Ensure all fire exit hardware is functional and tested; do not padlock emergency exits during occupancy.
guidanceText: Physically test each emergency exit to confirm it opens freely from inside; check for padlocks or obstructions.

text: Are emergency and fire safety signs clearly visible, legible, and correctly located?
hazardStatement: Missing or unclear signs delay evacuation and reduce safe response to emergencies.
consequenceStatement: Confusion during evacuation, slower response, and potential injury.
recommendation: Inspect all emergency and fire safety signs regularly; replace faded, damaged, or incorrectly positioned signs.
guidanceText: Check directional exit signs, fire point signs, and assembly point signs for visibility and accuracy.

text: Are flammable and combustible materials stored and controlled appropriately to reduce fire risk?
hazardStatement: Improperly stored flammable materials significantly increase fire risk and potential severity.
consequenceStatement: Rapid fire spread, explosions, severe injury, and facility destruction.
recommendation: Store flammable materials in designated, ventilated areas; limit quantities to operational needs; use appropriate containers.
guidanceText: Check storage areas for correct labelling, ventilation, and segregation from ignition sources.

text: Are emergency access routes for fire service vehicles kept clear at all times?
hazardStatement: Blocked fire service access routes prevent timely attendance, allowing fires to escalate.
consequenceStatement: Delayed emergency response, greater damage, and potential fatalities.
recommendation: Mark and enforce fire service access routes; prohibit parking or storage that obstructs these routes.
guidanceText: Walk external access routes to confirm they are clear and adequately signposted.

text: Are visitors and contractors included in site emergency arrangements and evacuation procedures?
hazardStatement: Visitors and contractors unaware of procedures may not evacuate correctly and can hamper the response.
consequenceStatement: Delayed or incomplete evacuation and potential injury to non-regular site occupants.
recommendation: Provide emergency briefing to visitors and contractors upon arrival; include in induction materials.
guidanceText: Check visitor induction process; ask contractors whether they have been briefed on emergency arrangements.

text: Is there a documented and current emergency response plan for the warehouse/depot?
hazardStatement: Without a documented emergency response plan, personnel may not respond correctly during an emergency at the depot.
consequenceStatement: Delayed evacuation, injury, or fatality due to lack of clear emergency guidance in a high-risk environment.
recommendation: Develop, document, and review the depot emergency response plan at least annually; ensure it addresses depot-specific hazards.
guidanceText: Check that the plan is current, covers depot hazards (fuel, chemicals, racking, vehicles), and is available on-site.

text: Are fire and emergency procedures communicated to staff, contractors, and visitors?
hazardStatement: Depot workers unaware of procedures may not evacuate safely or respond correctly to fire or spill emergencies.
consequenceStatement: Delayed or disorderly evacuation, potential injury or fatality.
recommendation: Include emergency procedures in depot induction training and post them throughout the facility.
guidanceText: Ask a random depot worker to describe evacuation procedures; check records of induction training.

text: Are emergency contact details, alarm instructions, and key response information clearly displayed in relevant areas?
hazardStatement: Lack of visible emergency information in the depot delays response when every second counts.
consequenceStatement: Delayed emergency response and potential harm to depot personnel.
recommendation: Post emergency contacts and alarm instructions near entrances, control rooms, and in each storage zone.
guidanceText: Check key depot areas for clearly displayed, up-to-date emergency information.

text: Is a site evacuation plan in place, current, and available where required?
hazardStatement: Without a current evacuation plan, depot occupants may not know escape routes or assembly points specific to the depot layout.
consequenceStatement: Disorganised evacuation in a complex depot environment leading to injury or fatality.
recommendation: Post depot evacuation plans at entrances and throughout the facility; update whenever layout changes.
guidanceText: Verify plans are posted, include current depot layout with racking, exits, and assembly points.

text: Are designated assembly points identified, accessible, and known to personnel?
hazardStatement: Unknown or inaccessible assembly points lead to confusion and failed headcounts in depot emergencies.
consequenceStatement: Delayed accountability, potential rescue delays, and additional risk to emergency responders.
recommendation: Clearly mark assembly points, include them on depot evacuation plans, and confirm staff awareness during induction and drills.
guidanceText: Check assembly point signage is visible from depot exits; confirm staff can locate it without prompting.

text: Are fire wardens or marshals appointed, identifiable, and aware of their responsibilities where required?
hazardStatement: Without designated wardens, depot evacuation coordination and area sweeps may not occur.
consequenceStatement: Incomplete evacuation, unaccounted personnel, and delayed emergency response.
recommendation: Appoint and train fire wardens for each depot area; issue identifying vests; review appointments when personnel change.
guidanceText: Check appointment records and confirm wardens know their duties and which areas they cover.

text: Have fire drills or emergency evacuation drills been carried out and recorded at appropriate intervals?
hazardStatement: Untested evacuations in a large depot are slower and more likely to result in injury or unaccounted personnel.
consequenceStatement: Poor evacuation performance, potential injury, and regulatory non-compliance.
recommendation: Conduct and record at least one full depot fire drill annually; review outcomes and correct any deficiencies.
guidanceText: Check drill records for date, participation, outcomes, evacuation time, and corrective actions raised.

text: Are fire extinguishers provided where required, suitable for the identified risks, and easily accessible?
hazardStatement: Missing or wrong-type extinguishers leave depot workers unable to tackle incipient fires safely.
consequenceStatement: Fire escalation in a stock-dense environment, potential fatalities, and significant property damage.
recommendation: Conduct a depot fire risk assessment; position appropriate extinguisher types throughout the facility; ensure clear access.
guidanceText: Verify extinguisher type matches depot risks (Class A, B, CO₂ for electrical etc.); check for obstructions.

text: Are fire extinguishers inspected, serviced, and within valid maintenance or test dates?
hazardStatement: Unserviced extinguishers may fail when needed in the depot, rendering fire-fighting attempts ineffective.
consequenceStatement: Inability to control incipient depot fire, leading to escalation and injury.
recommendation: Service all depot extinguishers annually; check tags monthly; replace out-of-date units immediately.
guidanceText: Check service tags; confirm last service date and next due date are current for all extinguishers in the depot.

text: Are fire alarm systems, manual call points, detectors, and warning devices present and in working order?
hazardStatement: Faulty or absent alarm systems mean fires in the depot may not be detected in time to permit safe evacuation.
consequenceStatement: Delayed or failed evacuation, fatalities, and severe stock and property loss.
recommendation: Test depot alarm systems regularly; maintain service records; replace faulty components immediately.
guidanceText: Check alarm test records; inspect call points and detectors throughout the depot for damage or obstruction.

text: Can alarms be clearly heard or otherwise effectively detected in offices, workshops, stores, and yard areas?
hazardStatement: Alarms that cannot be heard in noisy depot areas leave workers unaware of an emergency.
consequenceStatement: Delayed evacuation and potential injury to workers in areas with poor alarm coverage.
recommendation: Test audibility in all depot areas; add visual or vibrating alarms in high-noise zones; survey yard areas.
guidanceText: Walk all depot areas including yard during alarm test; verify coverage with additional sounders or visual devices where needed.

text: Are escape routes and emergency exits clearly marked, unobstructed, and immediately usable?
hazardStatement: Obstructed or unmarked exits in the depot prevent workers from escaping during a fire.
consequenceStatement: Trapped workers, fatalities, and regulatory non-compliance.
recommendation: Inspect all depot escape routes daily; ensure exits are signed, lit, and clear of stock, vehicles, and equipment.
guidanceText: Walk all depot escape routes; check for blocked aisles, missing exit signs, and absent emergency lighting.

text: Are emergency exit doors easy to open and not locked, blocked, or otherwise restricted during occupancy?
hazardStatement: Locked or blocked depot exits trap workers during a fire emergency.
consequenceStatement: Fatalities due to inability to escape and severe legal liability.
recommendation: Ensure all depot fire exit hardware is functional; never padlock emergency exits during occupancy.
guidanceText: Physically test each emergency exit in the depot to confirm it opens freely from inside; check for padlocks or obstructions.

text: Are emergency and fire safety signs clearly visible, legible, and correctly located?
hazardStatement: Missing or unclear fire safety signs delay evacuation in a large, complex depot environment.
consequenceStatement: Confusion during evacuation and potential injury.
recommendation: Regularly inspect depot fire and emergency signs; replace faded, damaged, or incorrectly positioned signs.
guidanceText: Check directional exit signs, fire extinguisher location signs, and assembly point signs throughout the depot.

text: Are flammable and combustible materials stored and controlled appropriately to reduce fire risk?
hazardStatement: Improperly stored flammable materials in the depot significantly increase fire risk and potential severity.
consequenceStatement: Rapid fire spread through stock, explosions, severe injury, and facility destruction.
recommendation: Store flammables in designated, ventilated areas; limit quantities to operational needs; ensure correct containers and labelling.
guidanceText: Check depot storage areas for correct labelling, ventilation, quantities, and segregation from ignition sources.

text: Are incompatible materials stored separately and not mixed where this could create fire, explosion, or dangerous reaction risks?
hazardStatement: Co-storage of incompatible materials can cause fire, explosion, or release of toxic gases.
consequenceStatement: Explosion, toxic release, fatalities, and catastrophic facility damage.
recommendation: Implement a depot chemical segregation plan based on SDSs; train staff on incompatibility; label storage areas clearly.
guidanceText: Check depot storage against a segregation matrix; verify incompatible materials are physically separated.

text: Are combustible materials and combustible waste controlled to minimise fire risk in buildings, workshops, storage areas, and yard areas?
hazardStatement: Accumulated combustible waste provides fuel for fire and increases severity.
consequenceStatement: Rapid fire spread in depot leading to greater damage, injury, and loss.
recommendation: Remove combustible waste regularly; keep aisles and yard areas free of cardboard, packaging, and debris.
guidanceText: Inspect depot aisles, yard, and skip areas for accumulation of combustible waste.

text: Is access to hazardous or high fire-risk areas restricted to authorised persons only?
hazardStatement: Unauthorised access to high fire-risk depot areas increases the probability of accidental ignition.
consequenceStatement: Accidental fire, explosion, or exposure of untrained persons to serious hazards.
recommendation: Lock or control access to hazardous areas; use permit-to-work systems for high-risk tasks.
guidanceText: Check access controls at chemical stores, fuel areas, and battery charging stations.

text: Are 'No Smoking', 'No Naked Flames', and other required prohibition signs clearly displayed in relevant areas?
hazardStatement: Absence of prohibition signs increases the risk that ignition sources will be introduced into fire-hazardous areas.
consequenceStatement: Accidental ignition leading to fire, explosion, and injury.
recommendation: Post prohibition signs at all entrances to fuel stores, chemical areas, and other flammable-risk zones.
guidanceText: Inspect all fire-risk areas for correct, legible prohibition signage; check signs are not obscured.

text: Are ignition sources controlled and prohibited where flammable materials, fuels, gases, or vapours may be present?
hazardStatement: Uncontrolled ignition sources in the presence of flammable atmospheres can cause explosion or fire.
consequenceStatement: Explosion or flash fire causing fatalities and catastrophic damage.
recommendation: Implement a hot work permit system; prohibit naked flames and non-intrinsically-safe equipment in flammable zones.
guidanceText: Check whether hot work permits are in use; verify no ignition sources are present in flammable-material areas.

text: Are hazardous storage and fire-risk areas clearly identified with appropriate warning and fire safety signage?
hazardStatement: Unlabelled hazardous areas expose uninformed workers and emergency responders to unrecognised risk.
consequenceStatement: Accidental exposure, injury, and incorrect emergency response.
recommendation: Label all hazardous storage areas with appropriate GHS, NFPA, or local hazard signs; update when contents change.
guidanceText: Walk all depot storage and risk areas; check for current, correct hazard identification signage.

text: Are refuelling, battery charging, welding, cutting, and other high fire-risk activities controlled appropriately?
hazardStatement: High fire-risk activities without controls are a leading cause of depot fires and explosions.
consequenceStatement: Fire, explosion, severe injury, and significant property and stock loss.
recommendation: Use hot work permits; control refuelling and charging in designated areas; train staff on hazards and safe procedures.
guidanceText: Check permit records for hot work; inspect refuelling and charging areas for compliance with controls.

text: Are spill kits, absorbents, and containment materials available where fuels, oils, or chemicals could create emergency risk?
hazardStatement: Uncontrolled fuel or chemical spills create fire and environmental hazards.
consequenceStatement: Spill-initiated fire, environmental contamination, and regulatory non-compliance.
recommendation: Place appropriate spill kits near fuel and chemical storage; inspect regularly; train staff on use.
guidanceText: Verify spill kits are present, correctly stocked, and accessible near all fuel and chemical storage areas.

text: Are emergency access routes for fire service or rescue vehicles kept clear at all times?
hazardStatement: Blocked emergency vehicle access routes prevent timely attendance at depot fires.
consequenceStatement: Delayed emergency response allowing fires to escalate, causing greater damage and potential fatalities.
recommendation: Mark and enforce fire service access routes with yellow lines and signs; prohibit vehicle parking or stock placement on these routes.
guidanceText: Walk depot perimeter and internal roads; confirm access routes are marked, signed, and clear.

text: Are visitors, contractors, and other non-routine occupants covered by emergency arrangements and evacuation procedures?
hazardStatement: Visitors and contractors unfamiliar with depot layout may not evacuate correctly and can obstruct the response.
consequenceStatement: Delayed or incomplete evacuation and potential injury to non-regular depot occupants.
recommendation: Brief visitors and contractors on depot emergency procedures upon arrival; include depot-specific hazards in induction.
guidanceText: Check depot visitor/contractor induction process; ask arriving contractors whether they have been briefed.

text: Are washroom facilities available, clean, hygienic, and in working condition?
hazardStatement: Unclean or non-functional washrooms pose hygiene and health risks to all personnel.
consequenceStatement: Spread of illness, regulatory non-compliance, and staff welfare complaints.
recommendation: Implement a cleaning schedule; inspect washrooms daily; repair faults promptly.
guidanceText: Check cleanliness, functionality of toilets, locks, and overall hygiene standards.

text: Are handwashing facilities available, functional, and supplied with water, soap, and means for drying hands?
hazardStatement: Lack of handwashing facilities increases risk of cross-contamination and spread of illness.
consequenceStatement: Spread of infectious disease and regulatory non-compliance.
recommendation: Ensure sinks are functional with running water, soap dispensers, and paper towels or hand dryers at all times.
guidanceText: Check soap dispensers, water supply, and drying facilities; confirm consumables are stocked.

text: Are suitable waste bins provided in washrooms and emptied regularly?
hazardStatement: Overflowing waste bins create unhygienic conditions and health risks.
consequenceStatement: Poor hygiene, unpleasant conditions, and potential infection risk.
recommendation: Provide adequate covered waste bins; include in daily cleaning rounds.
guidanceText: Check bins are present, covered where needed, and not overflowing.

text: Are washrooms adequately lit, ventilated, and comfortable for use?
hazardStatement: Poor lighting and ventilation make washrooms uncomfortable and may create slip hazards.
consequenceStatement: Staff discomfort, hygiene issues, and potential accidents in poorly lit areas.
recommendation: Ensure lighting is adequate; check that ventilation systems are operational and remove odours effectively.
guidanceText: Check lighting levels and ventilation fans or windows; note any damp or condensation issues.

text: Are washrooms accessible to all relevant personnel, including visitors and contractors where required?
hazardStatement: Inaccessible washrooms deny basic welfare to some personnel and may breach legal requirements.
consequenceStatement: Regulatory non-compliance and welfare complaints.
recommendation: Ensure washrooms are accessible to all site personnel; provide accessible facilities for people with disabilities.
guidanceText: Check accessibility for visitors, contractors, and personnel with disabilities.

text: Are separate washroom facilities provided for male and female personnel where required?
hazardStatement: Failure to provide separate facilities where required breaches welfare and dignity regulations.
consequenceStatement: Regulatory non-compliance, dignity concerns, and staff complaints.
recommendation: Provide separate male and female washrooms in accordance with applicable regulations and workforce size.
guidanceText: Confirm that facilities are correctly designated and available to all genders as required.

text: Are cleaning materials, consumables, and supplies for washrooms available and replenished as needed?
hazardStatement: Depleted supplies lead to unhygienic conditions and staff dissatisfaction.
consequenceStatement: Poor hygiene and welfare complaints.
recommendation: Maintain a stock of cleaning supplies and consumables; check levels during daily cleaning rounds.
guidanceText: Check that toilet paper, soap, and cleaning materials are stocked and not depleted.

text: Are drains, sinks, taps, flushing systems, and other washroom fittings functioning properly?
hazardStatement: Faulty fittings create unhygienic conditions, slip hazards from water accumulation, and welfare deficiencies.
consequenceStatement: Hygiene issues, flooding risk, and regulatory non-compliance.
recommendation: Inspect washroom fittings regularly; report and repair faults promptly.
guidanceText: Test taps, flush mechanisms, and drains; check for leaks, blockages, or standing water.

text: Is potable drinking water available, accessible, and clearly identified?
hazardStatement: Absence of potable water risks dehydration and associated health problems.
consequenceStatement: Dehydration, health issues, and regulatory non-compliance.
recommendation: Provide clearly labelled drinking water points accessible to all personnel; check water quality where required.
guidanceText: Confirm drinking water is available, labelled, and accessible throughout the facility.

text: Are meal facilities available, clean, suitable for the number of persons using them, and separated from work areas where contamination or hazardous exposure could occur?
hazardStatement: Eating in contaminated work areas exposes personnel to hazardous substances.
consequenceStatement: Ingestion of hazardous materials, illness, and regulatory non-compliance.
recommendation: Provide designated, clean meal areas separated from work activities; enforce no-eating rules in hazardous zones.
guidanceText: Check that a designated clean eating area is available, maintained, and separated from any hazardous areas.

text: Are lockers, changing areas, or other suitable personal storage facilities available where required?
hazardStatement: Absence of personal storage facilities may result in contamination of personal clothing or belongings.
consequenceStatement: Contamination of personal property and staff welfare complaints.
recommendation: Provide secure lockers or storage for personal items; ensure changing areas are clean and accessible where required.
guidanceText: Confirm lockers or equivalent storage are available and in usable condition.

text: Are first aid kits available, accessible, and clearly identified?
hazardStatement: Inaccessible or absent first aid kits delay treatment of injuries, worsening outcomes.
consequenceStatement: Delayed first aid, injury escalation, and potential fatality.
recommendation: Position first aid kits in prominent, accessible locations; mark clearly with signage; log locations on site maps.
guidanceText: Check that kits are available at required locations, clearly signed, and easily reachable.

text: Are first aid kits adequately stocked and within expiry dates where applicable?
hazardStatement: Inadequately stocked or expired first aid supplies cannot provide effective treatment.
consequenceStatement: Inability to provide adequate first aid, leading to worsening of injury.
recommendation: Inspect and restock kits monthly; replace expired items immediately; maintain inspection records.
guidanceText: Open kits and verify contents against required list; check expiry dates on dressings, wipes, and medications.

text: Are trained first aiders or appointed persons available and known to staff?
hazardStatement: Absence of trained first aiders means injuries cannot be treated promptly and correctly.
consequenceStatement: Worsening of injuries due to delayed or incorrect first aid, potential fatality.
recommendation: Appoint and train sufficient first aiders for the site; renew certificates before expiry; cover absences.
guidanceText: Confirm first aiders are available during working hours; ask staff whether they know who the first aider is.

text: Are the names and contact details of first aiders clearly displayed?
hazardStatement: Without displayed first aider contact details, staff may not know who to contact in an emergency.
consequenceStatement: Delayed first aid response and worsening of injury.
recommendation: Display first aider names, photos where possible, and contact details on noticeboards and near first aid kits.
guidanceText: Check noticeboards and kit locations for up-to-date first aider information.

text: Are eyewash stations or emergency drench facilities provided where required and in working condition?
hazardStatement: Absence of eyewash or drench facilities where chemicals are used can result in serious eye or skin injury.
consequenceStatement: Permanent eye damage or severe chemical burns due to inadequate emergency decontamination.
recommendation: Install eyewash stations within 10 seconds travel of chemical hazard areas; test and flush weekly; service regularly.
guidanceText: Check for eyewash stations where chemicals are in use; verify they are functional and within service dates.

text: Are first aid arrangements suitable for the nature of the work and number of persons on site?
hazardStatement: Inadequate first aid provision for site size or risk level means serious injuries cannot be managed effectively.
consequenceStatement: Delayed or inadequate treatment of injuries, potential fatality.
recommendation: Conduct a first aid needs assessment; scale provision to workforce size, hazard level, and remoteness of site.
guidanceText: Review first aid needs assessment; confirm number of first aiders, kits, and facilities match requirements.

text: Are first aid records maintained where required?
hazardStatement: Absence of first aid records prevents trend analysis and may indicate under-reporting of incidents.
consequenceStatement: Missed opportunities to prevent recurrence and potential regulatory non-compliance.
recommendation: Maintain accident/first aid records; review periodically to identify trends; store securely.
guidanceText: Check that a first aid treatment record or accident book is available and entries are being made.

text: Are first aid supplies inspected and replenished regularly?
hazardStatement: Depleted first aid supplies leave the facility unable to respond adequately to injuries.
consequenceStatement: Inadequate treatment of injuries due to missing supplies.
recommendation: Establish a monthly inspection and replenishment schedule for all first aid kits; record inspections.
guidanceText: Check inspection records and physically verify kit contents are complete.

text: Is suitable PPE provided for the tasks and hazards present?
hazardStatement: Unsuitable PPE fails to protect personnel from identified hazards.
consequenceStatement: Injury or illness due to inadequate protection.
recommendation: Conduct a PPE needs assessment based on hazard identification; provide task-specific PPE to all required personnel.
guidanceText: Check that PPE type matches hazards; verify it meets applicable standards.

text: Is PPE in good condition, clean, and fit for use?
hazardStatement: Damaged or dirty PPE provides reduced or no protection.
consequenceStatement: Injury or illness due to ineffective PPE.
recommendation: Inspect PPE before each use; remove and replace defective items; maintain a cleaning schedule for reusable PPE.
guidanceText: Physically inspect PPE in use and in storage for cracks, damage, deterioration, or contamination.

text: Are employees trained in the correct use, care, limitations, and storage of PPE?
hazardStatement: Untrained users may wear PPE incorrectly, reducing its effectiveness or causing additional risk.
consequenceStatement: Injury due to incorrectly worn or used PPE.
recommendation: Provide PPE training during induction and when new PPE is introduced; keep training records.
guidanceText: Check training records; observe whether personnel are wearing PPE correctly.

text: Is PPE being worn correctly where required?
hazardStatement: Incorrectly worn PPE provides no or reduced protection even when the correct type is provided.
consequenceStatement: Injury or illness due to PPE misuse.
recommendation: Supervise PPE use; address non-compliance immediately; reinforce correct wearing technique.
guidanceText: Observe personnel in work areas; check that PPE is worn as required and fitted correctly.

text: Is defective or damaged PPE replaced promptly?
hazardStatement: Continued use of defective PPE exposes personnel to hazards without adequate protection.
consequenceStatement: Injury or illness due to failed or compromised PPE.
recommendation: Establish a clear process for reporting and replacing defective PPE; keep replacement stock available.
guidanceText: Ask staff how they report damaged PPE; check whether replacement stock is available.

text: Is PPE stored properly when not in use?
hazardStatement: Improper storage leads to PPE degradation, contamination, or loss.
consequenceStatement: Damaged or unavailable PPE when required.
recommendation: Provide designated clean storage for PPE; keep away from UV, chemicals, and physical damage.
guidanceText: Check PPE storage areas; verify items are hung, bagged, or boxed appropriately.

text: Are appropriate signs displayed where PPE must be worn?
hazardStatement: Absence of PPE mandatory signs means personnel may not know where protection is required.
consequenceStatement: Failure to wear PPE in mandatory areas, leading to potential injury.
recommendation: Post mandatory PPE signs at the entrance to all areas where PPE is required.
guidanceText: Check that PPE mandatory signs are present, visible, and correctly identify the required PPE type.

text: Are visitor and contractor PPE requirements identified and enforced where required?
hazardStatement: Visitors and contractors without PPE are exposed to site hazards and may create risks for others.
consequenceStatement: Injury to non-regular site occupants and regulatory non-compliance.
recommendation: Define and communicate PPE requirements for visitors and contractors; provide PPE at site reception where needed.
guidanceText: Check visitor induction process; confirm PPE is provided or required to be brought by contractors.
