text: Are SDS or other hazard information available where chemicals are used, and is PPE matched accordingly?
hazardStatement: Without SDS-matched PPE, personnel may be unprotected from specific chemical hazards.
consequenceStatement: Chemical exposure, illness, or injury due to incorrect PPE selection.
recommendation: Maintain SDS for all chemicals on-site; use SDS PPE requirements to specify correct protection; train users accordingly.
guidanceText: Check that SDS are available at chemical use points and that PPE matches SDS requirements.

text: Frequently used items are within easy access between knee and shoulder height
hazardStatement: Reaching above shoulder or below knee for frequent items causes musculoskeletal strain.
consequenceStatement: Back, shoulder and neck injuries.
recommendation: Reorganise storage to place frequent items in the power zone.
guidanceText: Observe pick tasks and check height of frequent-access items.

text: Heavy items are stored at waist height
hazardStatement: Lifting heavy items from floor or overhead height causes back injury.
consequenceStatement: Acute or chronic musculoskeletal disorders.
recommendation: Relocate heavy items to waist-height shelving.
guidanceText: Identify the heaviest items in storage and verify their shelf position.

text: Stepladders or safe steps are available to access items stored on high shelves
hazardStatement: Climbing shelving or using improvised steps causes falls.
consequenceStatement: Falls from height resulting in fractures or head injury.
recommendation: Provide and enforce use of appropriate steps for high-shelf access.
guidanceText: Confirm steps are available and workers are using them.

text: Trolleys are available for heavy items and loads; adequate space is available for manual handling techniques or mechanical aids
hazardStatement: Carrying heavy loads manually without aids causes musculoskeletal injury.
consequenceStatement: Acute back injury and long-term musculoskeletal disorders.
recommendation: Provide trolleys and ensure aisles are wide enough for their use.
guidanceText: Check trolley availability and aisle width for safe operation.

text: Repetitive actions are minimised
hazardStatement: Repetitive movements cause cumulative musculoskeletal disorders.
consequenceStatement: Long-term injury and reduced worker capacity.
recommendation: Rotate tasks and introduce job variety to reduce repetition.
guidanceText: Observe task cycles and assess frequency of repeated movements.

text: HIRA has been carried out for manual handling tasks
hazardStatement: Unassessed manual handling tasks expose workers to uncontrolled risks.
consequenceStatement: Injuries that could have been prevented with proper controls.
recommendation: Complete manual handling risk assessments for all relevant tasks.
guidanceText: Request manual handling HIRA documents and verify currency.

text: Is electrical equipment tested and, where required, fitted with current test tags?
hazardStatement: Untested electrical equipment may have undetected faults that cause electric shock or fire.
consequenceStatement: Electric shock, fire, and regulatory non-compliance.
recommendation: Implement an electrical equipment testing programme; ensure test tags are current; keep records.
guidanceText: Check test tags on portable electrical equipment; verify dates are within required intervals.

text: Are electrical cords or cables, plugs, sockets, and electrical fittings in good condition with no visible damage?
hazardStatement: Damaged electrical cords, plugs, or sockets can cause electric shock, short circuit, or fire.
consequenceStatement: Electric shock, fire, and equipment damage.
recommendation: Inspect all cords, plugs, and sockets regularly; replace or repair damaged items immediately.
guidanceText: Visually inspect power cords and plugs for cracks, fraying, exposed wires, or burn marks.

text: Are electrical cords or cables routed safely and kept away from walkways, pinch points, and trip hazards?
hazardStatement: Cords across walkways create trip hazards and can be damaged by foot traffic, causing electric shock or fire.
consequenceStatement: Trip injury, electric shock, or cord damage leading to fire.
recommendation: Route cords along walls or use cable management covers; avoid trailing cords across pedestrian routes.
guidanceText: Walk through office areas and check for trailing cables across walkways or under door frames.

text: Are power boards used appropriately, with no double adaptors or piggybacking?
hazardStatement: Double adaptors and piggybacked power boards cause overloading, overheating, and fire.
consequenceStatement: Electrical fire, equipment damage, and potential fatality.
recommendation: Prohibit double adaptors and daisy-chaining of power boards; provide additional outlets where needed.
guidanceText: Check all power boards in office areas; look for double adaptors or power boards plugged into power boards.

text: Is faulty electrical equipment identified, removed from use, and tagged out?
hazardStatement: Continued use of faulty electrical equipment risks electric shock, fire, and injury.
consequenceStatement: Electric shock, fire, and serious injury or fatality.
recommendation: Implement a clear tag-out system for faulty equipment; train staff to report and tag defective items.
guidanceText: Check for equipment with damage or faults that has not been taken out of service; verify tag-out system is in use.

text: Are there enough power outlets of suitable rating to avoid overloading circuits or unsafe temporary arrangements?
hazardStatement: Insufficient outlets lead to overloading via multiple adaptors, increasing fire risk.
consequenceStatement: Overloaded circuits, fire risk, and equipment failure.
recommendation: Install additional outlets where demand is high; avoid relying on power boards as a permanent solution.
guidanceText: Count outlets versus devices in use; check for overloaded boards or temporary arrangements used long-term.

text: Are light fittings, switches, and power outlets in good working condition?
hazardStatement: Faulty fittings, switches, or outlets can cause shock, fire, or inadequate lighting leading to accidents.
consequenceStatement: Electric shock, fire, or accidents due to inadequate lighting.
recommendation: Report and repair faulty fittings promptly; include in routine maintenance programme.
guidanceText: Check for flickering lights, broken switches, scorched outlets, or missing covers throughout office areas.

text: Are circuit breakers, fuses, and main isolation switches accessible and identifiable where required?
hazardStatement: Inaccessible or unlabelled circuit breakers delay isolation during emergencies, increasing harm.
consequenceStatement: Inability to isolate power quickly in an emergency, leading to extended exposure to shock or fire.
recommendation: Label all circuits on distribution boards; ensure boards are accessible and unobstructed.
guidanceText: Check distribution board labelling; verify the board is accessible and not blocked by storage.

text: Is only approved electrical equipment used, with no unauthorised heaters or devices creating fire risk?
hazardStatement: Unauthorised heaters and high-draw devices overload circuits and create fire hazards.
consequenceStatement: Electrical fire, circuit overload, and potential fatality.
recommendation: Establish and enforce an approved equipment list; prohibit personal heaters and unapproved devices.
guidanceText: Check for personal heaters, toasters, or other high-draw unapproved devices plugged in around the office.

text: Are electrical hazard, warning, and prohibition signs displayed where required?
hazardStatement: Absent electrical safety signs leave personnel unaware of electrical hazards.
consequenceStatement: Accidental contact with electrical hazards due to lack of warning.
recommendation: Post appropriate electrical hazard and prohibition signs at distribution boards and hazardous electrical areas.
guidanceText: Check for hazard and prohibition signs at distribution boards, switch rooms, and electrical panels.

text: Are electrical rooms, switch rooms, or distribution board areas kept secure and accessible only to authorised persons?
hazardStatement: Unauthorised access to electrical rooms risks electric shock, burns, and fatality.
consequenceStatement: Electrocution or serious burns to unauthorised persons.
recommendation: Lock all electrical rooms and switch rooms; restrict keys or access to authorised and competent persons only.
guidanceText: Check that electrical rooms are locked; verify access is limited to trained and authorised personnel.

text: Are electrical panels and distribution boards kept clear, unobstructed, and accessible for safe operation and isolation?
hazardStatement: Obstructed distribution boards prevent safe isolation during faults or emergencies.
consequenceStatement: Delayed isolation in an emergency, increasing exposure to shock or fire.
recommendation: Maintain a 1-metre clear zone in front of all distribution boards; prohibit storage in these areas.
guidanceText: Inspect all distribution board areas for stored materials, furniture, or other obstructions.

text: Are electrical rooms or panel areas free from combustible storage, waste, water ingress, and other hazards?
hazardStatement: Combustibles or moisture in electrical rooms significantly increases the risk of electrical fire.
consequenceStatement: Electrical fire, arc flash, and serious injury or fatality.
recommendation: Remove all combustible materials from electrical rooms; inspect for water ingress and repair immediately.
guidanceText: Inspect electrical rooms for stored items, waste, damp, or water staining on walls and floors.

text: Is electrical equipment tested and, where required, fitted with current test tags?
hazardStatement: Untested electrical equipment in the depot may have undetected faults causing shock or fire.
consequenceStatement: Electric shock, fire, and regulatory non-compliance.
recommendation: Implement a testing programme for all portable electrical equipment in the depot; ensure tags are current.
guidanceText: Check test tags on tools, chargers, and portable equipment throughout the depot.

text: Are electrical cords or cables, plugs, sockets, wiring, insulation, and fixtures in good condition with no visible damage?
hazardStatement: Damaged wiring and fittings in the depot environment risk shock and fire, especially where vehicles and machinery operate.
consequenceStatement: Electric shock, fire, and equipment failure.
recommendation: Inspect all cords, plugs, sockets, and wiring regularly in depot areas; replace damaged items promptly.
guidanceText: Visually inspect cords and wiring in work areas, battery charging zones, and plant rooms.

text: Are electrical cords or cables kept off floors where possible and routed away from walkways, vehicle routes, wet areas, and mechanical damage?
hazardStatement: Cords on depot floors are at high risk of damage from vehicle traffic and create trip and shock hazards.
consequenceStatement: Cord damage, electric shock, trip injury, or fire.
recommendation: Overhead-route or protect cords in vehicle and pedestrian areas; use armoured cabling where mechanical damage is a risk.
guidanceText: Walk depot aisles and check for trailing cables on floors, especially in vehicle operating areas.

text: Are extension cords, portable electrical cables, and power boards serviceable and suitable for the environment in which they are used?
hazardStatement: Lightweight extension cords used in heavy-duty depot environments can overheat, fail, or cause shock.
consequenceStatement: Electric shock, fire, and equipment failure.
recommendation: Use industrial-rated extension cords and power boards rated for the depot environment; inspect before each use.
guidanceText: Check that extension cords in depot areas have sufficient rating for the load and environment.

text: Are power boards used appropriately, with no double adaptors or piggybacking?
hazardStatement: Double adaptors and chained power boards in the depot overload circuits and create fire risk.
consequenceStatement: Electrical fire, circuit overload, and potential fatality.
recommendation: Prohibit double adaptors and daisy-chaining power boards in all depot areas; install fixed outlets where needed.
guidanceText: Inspect all power boards and socket outlets in depot areas for double adaptors or chained boards.

text: Is faulty electrical equipment identified, removed from use, and tagged out?
hazardStatement: Continued use of faulty electrical equipment in the depot risks shock, fire, and serious injury.
consequenceStatement: Electric shock, fire, and serious injury or fatality.
recommendation: Implement a tag-out system for defective equipment; train depot staff to identify and report faults immediately.
guidanceText: Check for damaged equipment in service; verify that tag-out tags are available and being used.

text: Are there enough power outlets of suitable quantity and rating for the work being carried out?
hazardStatement: Insufficient outlets cause reliance on temporary or overloaded arrangements in the depot.
consequenceStatement: Overloaded circuits, fire risk, and equipment damage.
recommendation: Assess power demand in all depot work areas; install fixed outlets rated for industrial use where demand is high.
guidanceText: Check that power outlets are available and appropriately rated for equipment and machinery in use.

text: Are light fittings, switches, and power outlets serviceable and adequately protected against damage?
hazardStatement: Unprotected or damaged lighting and outlets in the depot can be struck by vehicles or equipment, causing shock or fire.
consequenceStatement: Electric shock, fire, or accidents due to inadequate lighting.
recommendation: Use industrial-grade fittings with appropriate IP ratings; protect outlets and fixtures from vehicle and forklift impact.
guidanceText: Inspect fittings for damage, missing covers, or signs of impact; check that lighting provides adequate coverage.

text: Are outlets, panels, circuit breakers, fuses, and main switches labelled and accessible for safe isolation?
hazardStatement: Unlabelled or inaccessible isolation points prevent safe isolation during emergencies in the depot.
consequenceStatement: Inability to isolate power quickly, extending exposure to shock or fire.
recommendation: Label all circuits and main switches; ensure all isolation points are accessible and unobstructed.
guidanceText: Check labelling on distribution boards; verify that isolation switches are reachable and clearly identified.

text: Are high-voltage panels, distribution boards, and control panels kept closed, secured, and protected from unauthorised access?
hazardStatement: Open or unsecured high-voltage panels in the depot expose all personnel to electrocution risk.
consequenceStatement: Electrocution or serious burns to workers or unauthorised personnel.
recommendation: Ensure all HV panels and distribution boards are closed, locked, and labelled; restrict access to authorised persons.
guidanceText: Check that all panels are closed and secured; confirm access is restricted to trained authorised personnel.

text: Is earthing visually sound and tested where required?
hazardStatement: Faulty earthing prevents fault current from being safely dissipated, increasing shock and fire risk.
consequenceStatement: Electric shock, electrocution, or fire from fault current.
recommendation: Include earthing in routine electrical inspections; test earth continuity at required intervals; record results.
guidanceText: Inspect earthing connections visually; check test records for earth continuity testing.

text: Is only approved electrical equipment used, with no unauthorised heaters or other devices creating fire risk?
hazardStatement: Unapproved high-draw or non-intrinsically-safe devices in the depot create overload and fire risk.
consequenceStatement: Electrical fire, circuit overload, and potential fatality.
recommendation: Enforce an approved equipment list in all depot areas; prohibit personal heaters and unapproved devices.
guidanceText: Look for personal heaters, unapproved chargers, or other non-approved devices in depot work areas.

text: Are electrical hazard, warning, mandatory, and prohibition signs displayed where required?
hazardStatement: Absent safety signs in the depot leave workers unaware of electrical hazards.
consequenceStatement: Accidental contact with electrical hazards due to insufficient warning.
recommendation: Post hazard, mandatory, and prohibition signs at all electrical panels, switch rooms, and hazardous equipment locations.
guidanceText: Inspect depot for required electrical signage at panels, switch rooms, and battery charging areas.

text: Are electrical installations and equipment protected from water, dust, impact, and other environmental damage where necessary?
hazardStatement: Electrical equipment exposed to water, dust, or impact in a depot environment can fail dangerously.
consequenceStatement: Electric shock, fire, and equipment failure in a high-use environment.
recommendation: Select electrical equipment with appropriate IP ratings for the environment; inspect for damage regularly.
guidanceText: Check IP ratings on electrical equipment used in wet, dusty, or high-impact areas; look for water ingress or damage.

text: Are temporary electrical arrangements controlled and used only where appropriate?
hazardStatement: Uncontrolled temporary electrical arrangements in the depot create overloading, trip, and fire hazards.
consequenceStatement: Electric shock, fire, and trip injury.
recommendation: Limit temporary arrangements to short-term use; use appropriate equipment; inspect and remove when no longer needed.
guidanceText: Identify any temporary electrical arrangements in the depot; check they are appropriate and controlled.

text: Are electrical rooms, switch rooms, and control rooms kept locked or otherwise secured against unauthorised access?
hazardStatement: Unsecured electrical rooms in the depot allow unauthorised access to high-voltage equipment.
consequenceStatement: Electrocution or serious burns to unauthorised persons.
recommendation: Lock all electrical and switch rooms; restrict access to authorised and competent persons only.
guidanceText: Check that all electrical rooms in the depot are locked; verify access control is in place.

text: Are electrical panels, switchboards, and distribution boards clearly identified and kept unobstructed for safe access and isolation?
hazardStatement: Obstructed or unlabelled distribution boards prevent safe isolation during emergencies in the depot.
consequenceStatement: Delayed isolation during an emergency, increasing exposure to shock or fire.
recommendation: Maintain a clear zone in front of all depot distribution boards; label all boards and circuits.
guidanceText: Check all depot distribution boards for obstructions, storage, or vehicle parking in front of panels.

text: Are electrical rooms free from stored materials, combustible items, waste, leaks, and other conditions that could create fire or electrical risk?
hazardStatement: Combustibles or moisture in depot electrical rooms significantly increase fire and arc flash risk.
consequenceStatement: Electrical fire, arc flash, serious injury, and facility damage.
recommendation: Remove all combustible items from electrical rooms; inspect for water ingress and repair immediately.
guidanceText: Inspect depot electrical rooms for stored items, waste, damp patches, or water staining.

text: Are warning signs, arc flash signs, or other required electrical safety signs displayed at electrical rooms and panels where required?
hazardStatement: Absent arc flash or high-voltage warning signs leave workers unaware of the severity of electrical hazards at depot panels.
consequenceStatement: Electrocution or severe arc flash burns to uninformed workers or contractors.
recommendation: Post arc flash, high-voltage, and danger signs at all electrical rooms and major distribution panels.
guidanceText: Check that arc flash and HV warning signs are present and legible at all electrical rooms and panels in the depot.

text: Are applicable SOPs available and displayed where required?
hazardStatement: Absent or inaccessible SOPs mean personnel cannot reference safe procedures when needed.
consequenceStatement: Non-standard or unsafe work practices leading to injury or regulatory non-compliance.
recommendation: Post relevant SOPs at the point of use; ensure they are accessible in both physical and digital format.
guidanceText: Check that SOPs are displayed or accessible at workstations and task areas where they are relevant.

text: Are personnel working in the area following the applicable SOPs?
hazardStatement: Non-compliance with SOPs increases risk of injury and introduces inconsistency in safe work practices.
consequenceStatement: Injury, process failure, or regulatory non-compliance due to deviation from approved procedures.
recommendation: Observe work being performed and compare against SOPs; address deviations through coaching and corrective action.
guidanceText: Observe personnel performing tasks and check compliance with relevant SOPs during the inspection.

text: Are work instructions available for critical or high-risk tasks?
hazardStatement: Absence of work instructions for high-risk tasks leaves personnel without clear guidance, increasing the likelihood of error.
consequenceStatement: Injury or incident due to incorrect performance of critical tasks.
recommendation: Identify all critical and high-risk tasks; develop and maintain work instructions; make them available at the point of use.
guidanceText: Check that work instructions exist for identified critical tasks such as chemical handling, equipment isolation, or working at height.

text: Are SOPs and work instructions current, approved, and reviewed at required intervals?
hazardStatement: Outdated or unapproved SOPs may contain incorrect procedures that create risk.
consequenceStatement: Personnel following outdated instructions, leading to injury or regulatory non-compliance.
recommendation: Establish a document control system with regular review cycles; ensure SOPs carry approval signatures and version numbers.
guidanceText: Check version numbers, approval signatures, and review dates on available SOPs and work instructions.

text: Is a current HIRA / risk assessment available for the work activities being carried out?
hazardStatement: Without a current risk assessment, hazards may be unidentified and controls absent.
consequenceStatement: Uncontrolled hazards leading to injury, illness, or incident.
recommendation: Conduct and document HIRAs for all significant work activities; review when activities, personnel, or conditions change.
guidanceText: Request and review the HIRA for activities observed during the inspection; check dates and approval.

text: Are emergency procedures documented and available where required?
hazardStatement: Undocumented or inaccessible emergency procedures delay correct response during an emergency.
consequenceStatement: Ineffective emergency response leading to increased harm, injury, or fatality.
recommendation: Document emergency procedures for all identified scenarios; post in key locations; ensure personnel are trained.
guidanceText: Check that emergency procedures are documented, posted where required, and current.

text: Are required licences, permits, certificates, or statutory documents available and current, including fire certificate where applicable?
hazardStatement: Operating without required licences or certificates breaches statutory obligations and indicates uncontrolled risk.
consequenceStatement: Regulatory non-compliance, facility closure, and reputational damage.
recommendation: Maintain a register of all statutory licences and certificates; set reminders for renewal before expiry.
guidanceText: Check fire certificates, operating licences, and other statutory documents for currency and validity.

text: Are Safety Data Sheets (SDS) available, current, and accessible for hazardous substances used or stored on site?
hazardStatement: Absence of SDS means personnel lack critical hazard, PPE, and emergency response information for chemicals in use.
consequenceStatement: Incorrect handling, inadequate PPE selection, and ineffective emergency response to chemical incidents.
recommendation: Maintain a current SDS register for all chemicals on-site; store SDS at the point of use; update when products change.
guidanceText: Check that SDS are available at chemical storage and use points; verify they are current (within 5 years).

text: Are training, induction, or competency records maintained for relevant personnel?
hazardStatement: Absence of training records makes it impossible to verify that personnel are competent to perform their tasks safely.
consequenceStatement: Unqualified personnel performing tasks, leading to error, injury, or regulatory non-compliance.
recommendation: Maintain a training register for all staff; record induction, task-specific, and statutory training; review for gaps.
guidanceText: Request training records for a sample of personnel; check induction and task-specific training has been completed.

text: Are incident reporting and corrective action procedures documented and available where required?
hazardStatement: Without clear incident reporting procedures, incidents may go unreported and corrective actions not taken.
consequenceStatement: Repeat incidents, unresolved hazards, and missed opportunities for improvement.
recommendation: Document and communicate incident reporting procedures; ensure forms and contact details are accessible to all staff.
guidanceText: Check that incident reporting procedures are documented and that personnel know how to report an incident.

text: Are contractor and visitor safety instructions or induction records available where required?
hazardStatement: Contractors and visitors without safety induction may be unaware of site hazards and emergency procedures.
consequenceStatement: Injury to non-regular site occupants and regulatory non-compliance.
recommendation: Maintain contractor and visitor induction records; include site-specific hazards, PPE requirements, and emergency procedures.
guidanceText: Check induction records for contractors and visitors currently on site; verify induction content covers key risks.

text: Work surfaces are set up at appropriate height for tasks undertaken
hazardStatement: Incorrect workstation height causes musculoskeletal strain.
consequenceStatement: Chronic neck, back or shoulder injury.
recommendation: Adjust surfaces to suit the task and the user.
guidanceText: Observe workers at their stations and check for awkward postures.

text: Layout of work area is suitable for tasks and reduces bending, twisting and overreaching
hazardStatement: Poor layout forces awkward postures during work.
consequenceStatement: Musculoskeletal disorders from sustained awkward positions.
recommendation: Redesign layout to minimise reach and awkward movement.
guidanceText: Note any tasks requiring frequent bending or overreaching.

text: Heavy or frequently used items are stored at waist height
hazardStatement: Incorrect storage height increases manual handling injury risk.
consequenceStatement: Back and shoulder injury during lifting.
recommendation: Relocate heavy items to between knee and shoulder height.
guidanceText: Check racking and shelving for placement of heavy items.

text: Persons are protected from sharp objects
hazardStatement: Exposed sharp edges cause lacerations.
consequenceStatement: Cuts and puncture wounds to hands and limbs.
recommendation: Cap or guard exposed sharp objects in work areas.
guidanceText: Inspect workstation edges, equipment and open packaging.

text: Free standing fittings are secure and stable
hazardStatement: Unstable fittings can fall and strike workers.
consequenceStatement: Impact injuries from falling equipment.
recommendation: Anchor all free-standing equipment and fittings.
guidanceText: Test stability of shelves and free-standing units.

text: Serviceability of office equipment is appropriate
hazardStatement: Faulty office equipment poses electrical and ergonomic hazards.
consequenceStatement: Electric shock or musculoskeletal injury.
recommendation: Report and remove defective equipment for repair.
guidanceText: Check for visible damage or malfunction in office equipment.

text: Windows are in a safe condition and where applicable operating appropriately
hazardStatement: Broken or improperly opening windows create cut and fall risks.
consequenceStatement: Lacerations or falls from height.
recommendation: Report and repair damaged windows promptly.
guidanceText: Check for cracked glass, broken handles and window restrictors.

text: Adequate rest breaks are taken during repetitive tasks or those requiring sustained postures
hazardStatement: Lack of rest breaks during sustained tasks causes fatigue and injury.
consequenceStatement: Musculoskeletal disorders and reduced worker wellbeing.
recommendation: Implement and enforce a scheduled rest break policy.
guidanceText: Confirm supervisors monitor and enforce break schedules.

text: COVID protocols are maintained
hazardStatement: Lapsed COVID controls increase transmission risk.
consequenceStatement: Illness and absenteeism among staff.
recommendation: Reinstate hygiene stations and reinforce protocols.
guidanceText: Check for hygiene stations and observe compliance with protocols.

text: Storage areas are accessible with appropriate layout
hazardStatement: Poorly accessible storage increases handling risk.
consequenceStatement: Spills or injuries during retrieval of hazardous materials.
recommendation: Reorganise storage for clear and safe access to all materials.
guidanceText: Inspect hazardous material storage areas for accessibility.

text: Storage areas are clearly defined
hazardStatement: Undefined storage areas lead to incompatible materials being stored together.
consequenceStatement: Chemical reaction, fire or toxic release.
recommendation: Mark and sign all designated storage zones.
guidanceText: Check that storage zones are marked and materials are correctly segregated.

text: Substances are properly labelled
hazardStatement: Unlabelled substances prevent identification of hazards.
consequenceStatement: Accidental exposure to hazardous materials.
recommendation: Label all containers with substance name, hazard symbols and handling instructions.
guidanceText: Check every container in storage for legible and compliant labelling.

text: Material Safety Data Sheets (MSDS) are available to staff
hazardStatement: Absence of MSDS leaves workers uninformed of chemical hazards.
consequenceStatement: Incorrect handling leading to chemical exposure.
recommendation: Provide MSDS for all chemicals in accessible format.
guidanceText: Confirm staff know where MSDS are stored and can access them.

text: Ventilation in storage areas is adequate
hazardStatement: Poor ventilation causes accumulation of toxic or flammable vapours.
consequenceStatement: Vapour inhalation injury or explosion.
recommendation: Install or improve ventilation in chemical storage areas.
guidanceText: Check for natural or mechanical ventilation in all storage areas.

text: Personnel are aware of hazardous substances they work with and their proper handling and storage
hazardStatement: Uninformed workers handle chemicals incorrectly increasing exposure risk.
consequenceStatement: Chemical injury or illness.
recommendation: Conduct chemical handling training and verify understanding.
guidanceText: Ask staff to identify hazards and handling procedures for their chemicals.

text: All hazardous substance containers are labelled in accordance with WHMIS requirements or with appropriate hazardous waste labels
hazardStatement: Non-compliant labelling prevents correct hazard identification.
consequenceStatement: Accidental exposure from misidentified substances.
recommendation: Relabel all containers to meet WHMIS or waste label standards.
guidanceText: Check every container for compliant hazard labelling.

text: Warning signs are posted on entrances to storage areas and equipment consistent with hazards within
hazardStatement: Absent warning signs allow uninformed entry to hazardous areas.
consequenceStatement: Chemical exposure injury to uninformed persons.
recommendation: Post hazard warning signs at all storage area entrances.
guidanceText: Check entrances to all storage and laboratory areas for required signs.

text: Emergency response plans are available and reviewed by all personnel (fire, power outage, spill, etc.)
hazardStatement: Unknown emergency plans lead to ineffective response.
consequenceStatement: Injury or environmental damage from uncontrolled emergency.
recommendation: Review emergency plans with all staff and record acknowledgements.
guidanceText: Ask staff to describe the emergency procedure for a spill scenario.

text: Emergency contact information is posted and up-to-date
hazardStatement: Outdated contacts delay emergency response.
consequenceStatement: Delayed assistance worsening emergency outcomes.
recommendation: Update and repost emergency contact information.
guidanceText: Verify phone numbers on posted contact lists are current.

text: Hazardous material inventories are maintained (chemical, biological and radiological)
hazardStatement: Incomplete inventories prevent accurate emergency response planning.
consequenceStatement: Unidentified hazardous materials complicate emergency response.
recommendation: Maintain and regularly update a complete chemical inventory.
guidanceText: Request the chemical inventory and verify it matches materials on site.

text: Personnel know the location of and have access to up-to-date MSDS for all hazardous materials in their work area
hazardStatement: Inaccessible MSDS prevents correct emergency and handling response.
consequenceStatement: Delayed or incorrect treatment of chemical exposure.
recommendation: Make MSDS accessible at the point of use.
guidanceText: Ask workers to locate the MSDS for a chemical in their area.

text: Personnel know the nearest location of emergency shower, eyewash stations and fire extinguishers
hazardStatement: Ignorance of emergency equipment location delays decontamination.
consequenceStatement: Worsened chemical injury from delayed decontamination.
recommendation: Conduct emergency equipment location training.
guidanceText: Ask staff to point out the nearest eyewash station and extinguisher.

text: Chemical liquids are kept in sealed containers when not in use
hazardStatement: Open chemical containers release vapours and increase spill risk.
consequenceStatement: Vapour inhalation and chemical spill injury.
recommendation: Enforce policy of sealing all chemical containers when not in use.
guidanceText: Check chemical storage areas for open or improperly sealed containers.

text: Secondary containment is available for liquids not stored in approved storage cabinets
hazardStatement: Absence of secondary containment allows spill to spread uncontrolled.
consequenceStatement: Environmental contamination and slip hazard from spilled liquids.
recommendation: Provide bunded trays or cabinets for all liquid chemical storage.
guidanceText: Verify bunding is in place under all chemical liquid storage.

text: Risk assessment documentation is readily available for all lifting devices
hazardStatement: Unassessed lifting operations expose workers to uncontrolled hazards.
consequenceStatement: Serious injury from uncontrolled lifting failure.
recommendation: Ensure current risk assessments are in place and accessible.
guidanceText: Request HIRA documents for all lifting devices on site.

text: Equipment examination and certification is current and records are available
hazardStatement: Uncertified equipment may be in an unsafe condition.
consequenceStatement: Equipment failure causing injury or fatality.
recommendation: Ensure all equipment certification is current and on file.
guidanceText: Review examination certificates and check expiry dates.

text: Load rating is clearly labelled on all equipment
hazardStatement: Unknown load ratings lead to overloading and equipment failure.
consequenceStatement: Collapse or tip-over causing serious injury.
recommendation: Mark load ratings clearly on all lifting and handling equipment.
guidanceText: Check that SWL plates are present and legible on all equipment.

text: Serviceability of forklifts, cranes and other powered mobile equipment; equipment is in good operating condition
hazardStatement: Faulty powered equipment creates collision and tip-over risk.
consequenceStatement: Serious injury or fatality from equipment malfunction.
recommendation: Take defective equipment out of service and arrange immediate repair.
guidanceText: Inspect equipment for defects and review pre-use inspection records.

text: Personnel operating powered mobile equipment are using seatbelts or other restraining devices
hazardStatement: Unsecured operators are ejected during tip-over incidents.
consequenceStatement: Fatality or severe injury from operator ejection.
recommendation: Enforce mandatory seatbelt use and implement supervision checks.
guidanceText: Observe operators during use to confirm seatbelt compliance.

text: Equipment is inspected prior to each use and inspection records are available
hazardStatement: Uninspected equipment may have faults that develop into failures.
consequenceStatement: Equipment failure causing injury during operation.
recommendation: Implement and enforce pre-use inspection checklists.
guidanceText: Request pre-use inspection logbooks and check recency of entries.

text: Are operators of forklifts, reach trucks, and other powered mobile equipment trained, authorised, and competent?
hazardStatement: Untrained or unauthorised operators of powered mobile equipment are a leading cause of serious injuries.
consequenceStatement: Fatal or serious injury from collision, tip-over, or load drop caused by incompetent operation.
recommendation: Verify operator licences and authorisation records; ensure retraining at required intervals.
guidanceText: Check operator authorisation records; confirm valid licence or in-house competency assessment is current.

text: Are pre-use inspections completed on forklifts, reach trucks, and other powered mobile equipment before use, and are defects reported promptly?
hazardStatement: Defects not identified before use can cause equipment failure, tip-over, or loss of load control.
consequenceStatement: Serious injury or fatality from equipment failure during operation.
recommendation: Implement and enforce pre-shift inspection checklists; ensure defects are reported and equipment removed from service until repaired.
guidanceText: Check pre-use inspection records; confirm defect reporting and follow-up process is in place.

text: Is lifting equipment examination, certification, or other required inspection current, and are records available?
hazardStatement: Lifting equipment without current statutory examination may fail under load, causing fatal injury.
consequenceStatement: Fatality or serious injury from lifting equipment failure.
recommendation: Schedule statutory thorough examinations at required intervals; maintain records; act on defects immediately.
guidanceText: Check LOLER/statutory examination certificates and confirm next due dates are within schedule.

text: Are load ratings, capacities, and load charts clearly marked and available to the operator?
hazardStatement: Operating beyond rated capacity causes tip-over and structural failure of lifting equipment.
consequenceStatement: Equipment tip-over or collapse causing fatality or serious injury.
recommendation: Ensure load charts and capacity plates are affixed to equipment and legible; train operators to interpret them.
guidanceText: Check that capacity plates and load charts are present, legible, and understood by operators.

text: Are forklifts, reach trucks, and other powered mobile equipment maintained in safe operating condition and removed from service if defects affect safety?
hazardStatement: Poorly maintained powered mobile equipment can fail unexpectedly during operation.
consequenceStatement: Serious injury or fatality from brake, hydraulic, or structural failure.
recommendation: Follow manufacturer maintenance schedules; ensure safety-critical defects result in immediate removal from service.
guidanceText: Review maintenance records; check for any equipment currently tagged out or awaiting repair.

text: Are seatbelts or other operator restraint devices used where fitted?
hazardStatement: Operators not using seatbelts are at high risk of being crushed or ejected in a tip-over incident.
consequenceStatement: Fatality or serious crush injury in the event of equipment tip-over.
recommendation: Enforce mandatory seatbelt use on all fitted equipment; include in operator training and site rules.
guidanceText: Observe operators during use; confirm seatbelts are fastened and restraints are functioning.

text: Are pallet racking systems in sound condition, free from visible damage, and suitable for the loads being stored?
hazardStatement: Damaged or overloaded racking can collapse, causing fatalities and significant stock loss.
consequenceStatement: Racking collapse causing fatality, serious injury, and major property damage.
recommendation: Inspect racking regularly; tag and remove damaged components from service immediately; load within rated capacity.
guidanceText: Walk racking aisles and inspect uprights and beams for dents, bends, tears, or deformation.

text: Are safe working load notices or other load information clearly displayed on the racking system?
hazardStatement: Absence of load notices leads to overloading and structural failure of racking.
consequenceStatement: Racking collapse from overloading, causing serious injury or fatality.
recommendation: Display safe working load notices at the end of each racking bay; train operators to observe limits.
guidanceText: Check each racking bay for a legible safe working load notice with bay load and unit load information.

text: Is racking inspected routinely, with damage reported and corrective actions tracked?
hazardStatement: Undetected racking damage accumulates until catastrophic collapse occurs.
consequenceStatement: Racking collapse causing fatality, serious injury, and stock loss.
recommendation: Conduct weekly user inspections and annual expert inspections; log damage and track corrective actions to close-out.
guidanceText: Request inspection records; check that damage is logged, risk-classified, and corrective actions completed.

text: Are rack uprights, beams, rails, and frames protected from impact where lift trucks or reach trucks operate nearby?
hazardStatement: Vehicle impacts on unprotected racking components progressively weaken the structure until collapse.
consequenceStatement: Racking collapse from accumulated impact damage causing fatal or serious injury.
recommendation: Install upright protectors and end-of-aisle barriers; enforce low-speed zones in racking aisles.
guidanceText: Check uprights at aisle ends and corners for impact damage; verify protectors are in place and intact.

text: Are aisles, beam levels, and rack locations kept clear enough for safe reach-truck operation and pallet placement?
hazardStatement: Obstructed aisles force operators into unsafe manoeuvres that damage racking and cause accidents.
consequenceStatement: Racking impact, equipment damage, and potential injury from collision in congested aisles.
recommendation: Mark and enforce minimum aisle widths; prohibit storage of materials in aisles; keep beam levels free of overhanging loads.
guidanceText: Walk all racking aisles; check for obstructions, overhanging loads, and adequate clearance for reach-truck operation.

text: Are pallets used in the racking system suitable, stable, and in good condition for the storage method?
hazardStatement: Damaged or unsuitable pallets can collapse under load, causing rack failure and falling stock.
consequenceStatement: Stock collapse, racking damage, and potential serious injury from falling pallets.
recommendation: Inspect pallets before loading into racks; remove damaged, broken, or non-standard pallets from use.
guidanceText: Inspect pallets in use and in storage for broken boards, missing blocks, or structural damage.

text: Is the pallet shuttle system in good condition, with rails, lane components, shuttle units, and associated parts free from visible damage?
hazardStatement: Damaged shuttle rails or components can cause shuttle failure or pallet collapse within the lane.
consequenceStatement: Pallet collapse, stock damage, and potential injury to nearby workers.
recommendation: Inspect shuttle rails, lane components, and shuttle units regularly; remove damaged items from service.
guidanceText: Check shuttle rails and lane components for bends, cracks, deformation, or missing parts.

text: Are only trained and authorised personnel operating the shuttle system and its controls?
hazardStatement: Unauthorised or untrained operation of the shuttle system creates serious risk of pallet collapse or equipment damage.
consequenceStatement: Pallet collapse, stock damage, and injury from incorrect shuttle operation.
recommendation: Restrict shuttle system operation to trained and authorised personnel; maintain training and authorisation records.
guidanceText: Check training and authorisation records for shuttle system operators; confirm restriction to authorised personnel.

text: Are shuttle lanes and operating zones protected from unauthorised entry during loading, unloading, or shuttle movement?
hazardStatement: Unauthorised entry into shuttle lanes during operation creates a fatal crush risk.
consequenceStatement: Fatal or serious crush injury from pallet or shuttle movement in the lane.
recommendation: Implement exclusion zones around shuttle operating areas; use barriers, signage, and interlocks where available.
guidanceText: Check that exclusion zones are established and enforced during shuttle loading and unloading operations.

text: Are pallets used with the shuttle system suitable, consistent, and in good condition for the lane and rail arrangement?
hazardStatement: Non-standard or damaged pallets can jam or collapse in shuttle lanes, causing system failure.
consequenceStatement: Shuttle system damage and potential collapse of pallets within the lane.
recommendation: Use only pallets that meet the manufacturer's specifications for the shuttle system; inspect before loading.
guidanceText: Check that pallets in use meet system specifications and are free from damage that could cause lane obstruction.

text: Are shuttle batteries, remote controls, indicators, and charging arrangements maintained and functioning properly where applicable?
hazardStatement: Faulty batteries or controls can cause unpredictable shuttle movement or unexpected stoppage within a lane.
consequenceStatement: Equipment damage or injury from uncontrolled shuttle movement.
recommendation: Maintain shuttle batteries and remote controls per manufacturer schedule; check charging stations and indicators regularly.
guidanceText: Check battery condition, remote control function, and charging station serviceability for all shuttle units.

text: Are SOPs or work instructions available for shuttle loading, unloading, fault response, and recovery procedures?
hazardStatement: Without documented procedures, workers may attempt unsafe improvised recovery of shuttle faults.
consequenceStatement: Serious injury from unsafe access to shuttle lanes or incorrect fault recovery.
recommendation: Document and communicate SOPs for all shuttle system operations including fault response and recovery.
guidanceText: Check that SOPs are available at the shuttle system control point and cover loading, unloading, and fault recovery.

text: Are machine guards and other safety devices in place, adequate, and functioning properly?
hazardStatement: Missing or defective guards expose operators and bystanders to entanglement, cutting, or crushing hazards.
consequenceStatement: Serious injury or fatality from contact with unguarded moving parts.
recommendation: Inspect guards before each shift; remove machines from service immediately if guards are missing or defective.
guidanceText: Check that all guards are fitted, intact, and functional; verify interlocks stop the machine when guards are opened.

text: Are operating controls clearly identified, safe to use, and protected from inadvertent operation where required?
hazardStatement: Unidentified or unprotected controls risk inadvertent activation causing unexpected machine movement.
consequenceStatement: Serious injury from unexpected machine start or movement.
recommendation: Label all controls clearly; fit guards or recessed covers on critical controls; ensure emergency stops are prominently marked.
guidanceText: Check control labelling, emergency stop accessibility, and whether critical controls are protected from accidental activation.

text: Is plant and machinery inspected and maintained at suitable intervals, with records available where required?
hazardStatement: Poorly maintained plant and machinery fails unexpectedly, causing serious injury.
consequenceStatement: Equipment failure causing serious injury or fatality.
recommendation: Implement preventive maintenance schedules; maintain records; act on defects before returning to service.
guidanceText: Review maintenance records; check scheduled service dates and outstanding defect reports.

text: Is machinery stable, properly installed, and fit for the work being carried out?
hazardStatement: Unstable or incorrectly installed machinery can move, tip, or vibrate in ways that cause injury.
consequenceStatement: Injury from machinery movement, tipping, or vibration during operation.
recommendation: Ensure all machinery is correctly installed, anchored where required, and rated for the work being performed.
guidanceText: Inspect machinery foundations, fixings, and stability; check that equipment is rated for the task.

text: Are hazard zones around machinery clearly defined and protected against unintended access?
hazardStatement: Undefined or unprotected hazard zones allow bystanders to enter areas where they can be struck or caught by machinery.
consequenceStatement: Serious injury or fatality from entry into an active machine hazard zone.
recommendation: Mark hazard zones with floor markings, barriers, or safety fencing; use interlocked gates where required.
guidanceText: Check that hazard zones are clearly marked and physically protected; confirm bystander access is prevented during operation.

text: Have operators and maintainers been adequately instructed and trained in safe equipment use?
hazardStatement: Untrained operators and maintainers are significantly more likely to cause or be injured by machinery incidents.
consequenceStatement: Serious injury or fatality from incorrect operation or maintenance of machinery.
recommendation: Train all operators and maintainers before use; maintain training records; refresh training when equipment or procedures change.
guidanceText: Check training records for machine operators and maintainers; confirm training is current and covers safe use and emergency procedures.

text: Is machinery isolated or locked off before servicing, cleaning, adjustment, or maintenance?
hazardStatement: Failure to isolate machinery before maintenance is a leading cause of fatal and life-changing injuries.
consequenceStatement: Fatality or serious injury from unexpected machine start during maintenance or cleaning.
recommendation: Implement a lockout/tagout procedure; train all maintainers; audit compliance regularly.
guidanceText: Check whether a lockout/tagout procedure exists; observe or ask maintainers to describe the isolation process.

text: Are the correct hand tools or portable tools being used for the task?
hazardStatement: Using the wrong tool for a task increases the risk of slipping, breakage, and injury.
consequenceStatement: Laceration, puncture, or impact injury from incorrect tool use.
recommendation: Train workers to select the correct tool for each task; ensure adequate tool inventory is available.
guidanceText: Observe workers using hand tools; check that the correct tool type and size is being used for the task.

text: Are hand tools and portable tools kept in safe condition, and are defective tools removed from service?
hazardStatement: Defective hand tools can slip, break, or fail, causing laceration, puncture, or impact injury.
consequenceStatement: Laceration, puncture, or crush injury from tool failure or slipping.
recommendation: Inspect hand tools regularly; tag and remove defective tools from service; do not attempt field repairs of damaged tools.
guidanceText: Inspect hand tools in use and in storage for cracks, mushroomed heads, loose handles, or worn edges.

text: Are pneumatic hoses, compressed-air fittings, electrical cords, and related tool connections in good condition and safe to use?
hazardStatement: Damaged hoses, fittings, or cords can cause unexpected tool failure, whipping hoses, or electric shock.
consequenceStatement: Impact injury from whipping hose, electric shock, or tool failure.
recommendation: Inspect hoses, fittings, and cords before use; replace damaged items; use safety couplings on pneumatic hoses.
guidanceText: Check hoses, fittings, and power cords for cuts, abrasion, splits, or loose connections.

text: Have workers been trained or instructed in the safe use of hand and portable powered tools?
hazardStatement: Untrained workers using hand or power tools have a significantly higher risk of causing or sustaining injury.
consequenceStatement: Laceration, impact, or other tool-related injury due to incorrect technique.
recommendation: Provide tool-specific training during induction and when new tools are introduced; maintain training records.
guidanceText: Check training records for tool use; ask workers to describe safe use and common hazards of tools they operate.

text: Are safe work instructions or SOPs available where needed for higher-risk tools or tasks?
hazardStatement: Absence of work instructions for high-risk tool use increases the likelihood of incorrect and unsafe practices.
consequenceStatement: Injury from incorrect use of higher-risk tools or portable equipment.
recommendation: Develop and display SOPs for higher-risk portable tools such as angle grinders, nail guns, and pneumatic tools.
guidanceText: Check that SOPs or safe work instructions are available and accessible for higher-risk tools in use.

text: Correct types of hand tools are being used for the task
hazardStatement: Wrong tools for the task increase the risk of slipping and injury.
consequenceStatement: Lacerations or impact injuries from tool misuse.
recommendation: Verify workers are using tools appropriate for each task.
guidanceText: Observe tasks in progress and confirm correct tool selection.

text: Serviceability and condition of hand tools is satisfactory
hazardStatement: Defective hand tools are more likely to fail or slip causing injury.
consequenceStatement: Laceration or impact injury from tool failure.
recommendation: Remove and replace all defective hand tools from service.
guidanceText: Inspect a sample of hand tools for cracks, worn edges and loose handles.

text: Condition of air and electrical lines and fittings is satisfactory
hazardStatement: Damaged air or electrical lines create shock and whip hazards.
consequenceStatement: Electric shock or laceration from hose failure.
recommendation: Inspect and replace damaged lines and fittings.
guidanceText: Check air hoses and power leads for wear and secure connections.

text: Adequate instruction and training has been provided for use of tools
hazardStatement: Untrained tool users increase injury risk.
consequenceStatement: Laceration or injury from incorrect tool use.
recommendation: Ensure training records exist for all tool operators.
guidanceText: Request tool competency training records.

text: Risk assessment documentation is readily available for all tool risks
hazardStatement: Unassessed tool use exposes workers to uncontrolled risks.
consequenceStatement: Injury from uncontrolled tool hazards.
recommendation: Complete and display risk assessments for high-risk tools.
guidanceText: Check HIRA documents for power tools and grinding equipment.

text: Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) are up-to-date, readily accessible and enforced
hazardStatement: Outdated or ignored SOPs lead to unsafe tool use.
consequenceStatement: Injury from deviation from safe operating methods.
recommendation: Review, update and enforce SOPs for all tool operations.
guidanceText: Compare observed work methods with documented SOPs.

text: Adequate signage is in place for tool usage areas
hazardStatement: Missing signage leaves workers unaware of tool hazards in the area.
consequenceStatement: Injury from unrecognised tool hazard zone.
recommendation: Post appropriate warning signs in all tool use areas.
guidanceText: Check for PPE requirement signs and hazard warnings near tool storage.

text: Storage is designed to minimise manual handling problems (heavier and frequently used items between knee and shoulder height)
hazardStatement: Incorrectly placed items cause manual handling injuries.
consequenceStatement: Back and shoulder injuries from repeated awkward lifting.
recommendation: Reorganise storage to place heavy items in the power zone.
guidanceText: Check shelf heights for heaviest and most frequently accessed items.

text: Ergonomic chairs with adjustable height, back tilt and lumbar support are provided for workstations
hazardStatement: Non-ergonomic seating causes musculoskeletal disorders.
consequenceStatement: Chronic back and neck pain reducing worker capacity.
recommendation: Replace non-adjustable chairs with ergonomic alternatives.
guidanceText: Check chairs for height adjustment, lumbar support and back tilt function.

text: Desktop space is sufficient
hazardStatement: Insufficient desk space forces awkward reaches and clutter.
consequenceStatement: Musculoskeletal strain and reduced productivity.
recommendation: Clear desks and provide additional surface area where needed.
guidanceText: Check available workspace on desks and workbenches.

text: Desktop setup allows the user to sit directly in front of the computer monitor without twisting or bending
hazardStatement: Twisted or bent sitting posture causes neck and back injury.
consequenceStatement: Chronic musculoskeletal disorders of the neck and back.
recommendation: Adjust monitor position so user faces it directly.
guidanceText: Observe the angle between monitor and user's seated position.

text: Computer monitors are positioned away from direct glare (sunlight, reflections, etc.)
hazardStatement: Screen glare causes eye strain and headaches.
consequenceStatement: Eye fatigue reducing worker concentration and comfort.
recommendation: Reposition monitors or use blinds to eliminate glare.
guidanceText: Observe screens from the user's position to detect glare.

text: Computer screens are clear, legible and stable (no flickering)
hazardStatement: Flickering or unclear screens cause eye strain.
consequenceStatement: Eye fatigue and headaches reducing performance.
recommendation: Replace or repair any screens that flicker or display poorly.
guidanceText: View each screen in use and check for flicker or clarity issues.

text: Laptop docking stations and/or external keyboard, mouse and monitors are provided for laptop users
hazardStatement: Using laptops without accessories forces poor posture.
consequenceStatement: Neck and back musculoskeletal disorders in laptop users.
recommendation: Provide docking stations and external peripherals for regular laptop users.
guidanceText: Identify staff who regularly use laptops and check their setup.

text: Document holders are provided for those who regularly view documents while keying, positioned directly in front of the user
hazardStatement: Referring to flat documents while typing causes neck rotation injury.
consequenceStatement: Neck strain and musculoskeletal disorders.
recommendation: Provide document holders for staff who type from paper documents.
guidanceText: Observe data entry tasks and check document holder availability.

text: Footrests are available to those that require them
hazardStatement: Dangling feet create lower back strain when seated.
consequenceStatement: Lower back and leg discomfort during prolonged sitting.
recommendation: Provide footrests on request and during DSE assessments.
guidanceText: Ask workers if footrests are available and used where needed.

text: Ambient temperature is comfortable
hazardStatement: Uncomfortable temperature causes heat or cold stress.
consequenceStatement: Reduced concentration, fatigue and potential heat illness.
recommendation: Adjust HVAC or provide fans and heaters as required.
guidanceText: Ask workers about thermal comfort and check thermostat settings.

text: Lighting is adequate and lights are in good working order
hazardStatement: Inadequate lighting causes eye strain and accident risk.
consequenceStatement: Eye fatigue and increased error rate.
recommendation: Replace faulty bulbs and review lighting levels.
guidanceText: Check lux levels and confirm all fixtures are operational.

text: There is sufficient ventilation
hazardStatement: Insufficient ventilation causes stuffiness and contaminant build-up.
consequenceStatement: Respiratory discomfort and reduced air quality.
recommendation: Service HVAC and ensure fresh air supply meets occupancy requirements.
guidanceText: Assess airflow in enclosed offices and meeting rooms.

text: Walkways, stairs and aisles are in good condition with anti-slip tread on steps
hazardStatement: Damaged surfaces and missing anti-slip treads cause falls.
consequenceStatement: Slip and fall injuries on stairs and walkways.
recommendation: Repair damaged surfaces and replace worn anti-slip treads.
guidanceText: Inspect all stairways and main aisles during the walkthrough.

text: Electrical cords are adequately bundled and stored to prevent tripping and are in good repair
hazardStatement: Loose cords across walkways create trip and shock hazards.
consequenceStatement: Falls and potential electric shock from damaged leads.
recommendation: Use cable ties and trunking to bundle and route all cords.
guidanceText: Check under desks and across walkways for loose or trailing cords.

text: Electrical power points are in good repair and not overloaded
hazardStatement: Overloaded and damaged outlets create fire and shock risk.
consequenceStatement: Electrical fire or shock injury.
recommendation: Replace damaged outlets and reduce load on overloaded circuits.
guidanceText: Count devices on each outlet and check for physical damage.

text: Adequate storage space is available to prevent clutter
hazardStatement: Clutter creates trip hazards and fire loading.
consequenceStatement: Trip injuries and increased fire risk.
recommendation: Provide adequate storage and enforce clear desk policy.
guidanceText: Observe desk and floor areas for unnecessary clutter.

text: Filing cabinets and shelves are secured to avoid tipping over
hazardStatement: Unsecured cabinets tip when drawers are open or in seismic events.
consequenceStatement: Crush injury from toppling cabinet.
recommendation: Anchor tall cabinets and shelving to walls.
guidanceText: Check that tall filing cabinets are secured to walls.

text: Walkways, hallways and stairs are kept tidy and free from obstructions
hazardStatement: Obstructed walkways create trip hazards and impede evacuation.
consequenceStatement: Falls and delayed emergency egress.
recommendation: Enforce housekeeping standard in all communal circulation areas.
guidanceText: Walk all corridors, hallways and stairways and note any obstruction.

text: Personnel understand ergonomic hazards in the workplace and how to protect themselves from musculoskeletal injuries
hazardStatement: Unaware workers do not take steps to prevent musculoskeletal injury.
consequenceStatement: Preventable musculoskeletal disorders develop over time.
recommendation: Deliver ergonomic awareness training to all office-based staff.
guidanceText: Ask workers to name ergonomic hazards relevant to their tasks.

text: There is no indication from personnel of musculoskeletal injuries as a result of work activities
hazardStatement: Unreported musculoskeletal symptoms indicate hidden hazards.
consequenceStatement: Progression of treatable conditions to permanent injury.
recommendation: Encourage reporting of discomfort and conduct early intervention assessments.
guidanceText: Ask workers about aches, pains or discomfort related to work.

text: Equipment and tools are in good working condition and properly maintained
hazardStatement: Poorly maintained equipment forces awkward compensating postures.
consequenceStatement: Musculoskeletal disorders from compensating for equipment faults.
recommendation: Repair or replace defective tools and equipment promptly.
guidanceText: Check the condition of chairs, keyboards and other ergonomic aids.

text: Equipment and hand tools are appropriate for the worker and job task
hazardStatement: Ill-fitting tools cause awkward grip and musculoskeletal strain.
consequenceStatement: Hand, wrist and arm disorders from tool misfit.
recommendation: Match tools to the task requirements and user anthropometrics.
guidanceText: Ask workers whether tools feel comfortable and suit the task.

text: Work stations, surfaces and equipment are at an appropriate height for the type of work performed
hazardStatement: Incorrect surface height forces sustained awkward postures.
consequenceStatement: Musculoskeletal disorders of the back, neck and shoulders.
recommendation: Adjust workstation heights to suit each worker's requirements.
guidanceText: Measure or visually assess workstation heights against workers.

text: Chairs are appropriate for the task and in good condition
hazardStatement: Inappropriate seating causes postural discomfort.
consequenceStatement: Musculoskeletal disorders from prolonged poor sitting posture.
recommendation: Replace worn or non-adjustable chairs with suitable alternatives.
guidanceText: Check chair condition and adjustment range at each workstation.

text: No sustained or awkward posture is required during performance of work activities
hazardStatement: Sustained awkward postures cause musculoskeletal disorders.
consequenceStatement: Chronic musculoskeletal injury reducing worker capacity.
recommendation: Redesign tasks or workstations to eliminate sustained awkward postures.
guidanceText: Observe workers during tasks and note any prolonged awkward positions.

text: Personnel understand and practice good lifting techniques
hazardStatement: Poor lifting technique increases musculoskeletal injury risk.
consequenceStatement: Acute back injury from incorrect manual handling.
recommendation: Deliver manual handling training and reinforce correct technique.
guidanceText: Observe lifting tasks and verify workers use correct posture.

text: Heavy objects are stored on shelves at approximately waist height
hazardStatement: Lifting heavy items from floor or overhead height causes back injury.
consequenceStatement: Acute or chronic musculoskeletal disorders.
recommendation: Relocate heavy items to waist-height shelves.
guidanceText: Check racking for placement of heaviest stock or files.

text: Materials stored above shoulder height are secured so that they do not fall
hazardStatement: Unsecured overhead items fall and strike persons below.
consequenceStatement: Head and shoulder impact injury from falling materials.
recommendation: Secure or reposition items stored above shoulder height.
guidanceText: Inspect high shelves for items at risk of falling.

text: Materials being handled are of a manageable size and weight
hazardStatement: Oversized or overweight loads increase manual handling injury risk.
consequenceStatement: Acute back and shoulder injuries.
recommendation: Split loads or provide mechanical aids for heavy or awkward items.
guidanceText: Check the weight and dimensions of items being manually handled.

text: Lifting devices are available
hazardStatement: Absence of lifting aids forces manual handling of heavy loads.
consequenceStatement: Musculoskeletal injury from manual handling of heavy items.
recommendation: Provide appropriate mechanical lifting aids for heavy tasks.
guidanceText: Confirm trolleys, pallet jacks or hoists are available and serviceable.

text: Frequently used items are placed close at hand
hazardStatement: Reaching for frequently used items causes cumulative strain.
consequenceStatement: Musculoskeletal disorders of the shoulder and arm.
recommendation: Reorganise workstations to place frequent-use items within easy reach.
guidanceText: Check the reach distance for commonly used tools and documents.

text: Has a task- and vehicle-specific laboratory risk assessment been completed for the mobile laboratory?
hazardStatement: Without a specific risk assessment, hazards unique to the mobile lab environment may be unidentified and uncontrolled.
consequenceStatement: Uncontrolled hazards leading to injury, chemical exposure, or incident during mobile lab operations.
recommendation: Complete and review a task- and vehicle-specific risk assessment before mobile lab operations; update when activities or equipment change.
guidanceText: Request and review the mobile lab risk assessment; confirm it is current, approved, and covers transport and operational phases.

text: Are laboratory safety, hazardous waste, and emergency response training records maintained for mobile lab personnel?
hazardStatement: Untrained personnel in a mobile lab environment may not recognise hazards or respond correctly to spills, exposure, or emergencies.
consequenceStatement: Chemical exposure, injury, or ineffective emergency response due to inadequate training.
recommendation: Maintain training records for all mobile lab personnel covering chemical safety, hazardous waste, spill response, and emergency procedures.
guidanceText: Check training records for currency and coverage; confirm personnel have completed required training before working in the mobile lab.

text: Is a current inventory of chemicals, samples, gases, and hazardous materials maintained for the mobile lab?
hazardStatement: Without a current inventory, emergency responders and personnel cannot identify all hazardous materials present in the mobile lab.
consequenceStatement: Incorrect emergency response and unidentified chemical hazards in the event of an incident.
recommendation: Maintain and update a chemical and hazardous material inventory for the mobile lab; make it accessible to all personnel and emergency responders.
guidanceText: Request the chemical inventory; verify it reflects current contents and is accessible in or on the vehicle.

text: Are current SDS available and accessible for all chemicals carried or used in the mobile laboratory?
hazardStatement: Absence of SDS in the mobile lab leaves personnel without critical hazard, PPE, and emergency response information.
consequenceStatement: Incorrect chemical handling and inadequate emergency response due to missing safety information.
recommendation: Keep current SDS for all chemicals in the mobile lab; store in an accessible location inside the vehicle.
guidanceText: Check that an SDS folder or digital SDS system is present in the vehicle and covers all chemicals carried.

text: Are all chemical, sample, and waste containers clearly labelled and compatible with their contents?
hazardStatement: Unlabelled or incompatible containers risk misidentification, chemical reactions, leaks, and exposure.
consequenceStatement: Chemical exposure, fire, or environmental contamination from mislabelled or incompatible containers.
recommendation: Label all containers with contents, hazard information, and date; use only compatible materials for each substance.
guidanceText: Inspect all containers in the mobile lab for legible labels and compatibility between container material and contents.

text: Are chemicals stored according to compatibility and secured in suitable cabinets, bins, racks, or restraints during transit?
hazardStatement: Incompatible chemicals stored together or unsecured during transit can mix, react, or spill causing fire, explosion, or exposure.
consequenceStatement: Chemical fire, explosion, or exposure from unsecured or incompatible chemical storage during transit.
recommendation: Segregate chemicals by compatibility; use secured cabinets, bins, and restraints designed for vehicle transport.
guidanceText: Check chemical storage areas in the van for correct segregation and adequate restraint against movement during travel.

text: Are flammables prevented from being stored in non-approved refrigerators or other unsuitable equipment?
hazardStatement: Flammables stored in standard refrigerators can be ignited by internal sparks causing fire or explosion.
consequenceStatement: Fire or explosion from ignition of flammable vapours inside a non-approved refrigerator.
recommendation: Use only flammable-safe or explosion-proof refrigerators for storing flammable materials.
guidanceText: Check refrigerators in the mobile lab for 'flammable safe' or equivalent certification; confirm flammables are not stored in standard units.

text: Are compressed gas cylinders, where present, kept upright, capped when not in use, and properly secured during transport and operation?
hazardStatement: Unsecured or uncapped cylinders can fall and rupture, causing catastrophic gas release, fire, or explosion.
consequenceStatement: Catastrophic gas release, fire, explosion, or cylinder-missile injury.
recommendation: Secure cylinders in upright positions using brackets or chains; cap all cylinders when not in use; use appropriate cylinder trolleys during movement.
guidanceText: Check that cylinders are secured upright, capped when not connected, and restrained against movement during transit.

text: Is the vehicle parked, stabilized, and safe to occupy before laboratory work begins?
hazardStatement: Unsecured or unstable vehicles can move during laboratory work, causing spills, injuries, or chemical exposure.
consequenceStatement: Injury or chemical spill from vehicle movement during lab operations.
recommendation: Ensure the vehicle is parked on level ground, engine off, handbrake applied, and stabilisers deployed before commencing laboratory work.
guidanceText: Check that the vehicle is parked safely, stabilised, and engine off before personnel commence work inside.

text: Are benches, instruments, refrigerators, cylinders, and storage units secured to prevent movement during travel?
hazardStatement: Unsecured equipment and storage units in the mobile lab can shift during travel, causing damage, spills, or injury.
consequenceStatement: Equipment damage, chemical spill, or injury from shifting items during vehicle movement.
recommendation: Fit all fixed equipment with securing brackets, anti-vibration mounts, or restraints rated for vehicle movement.
guidanceText: Inspect the vehicle interior for adequate securing of benches, instruments, refrigerators, cylinders, and storage units.

text: Is ventilation adequate for the chemicals, samples, and procedures carried out inside the van?
hazardStatement: Inadequate ventilation in the mobile lab allows accumulation of vapours and fumes, creating health and fire risks.
consequenceStatement: Chemical vapour exposure causing illness, or ignition risk from accumulated flammable vapours.
recommendation: Ensure the mobile lab has adequate mechanical or natural ventilation; use local exhaust where fume-generating procedures are conducted.
guidanceText: Check ventilation systems in the van; assess whether ventilation is adequate for the chemicals and procedures being conducted.

text: Are power supplies, generators, external hookups, and electrical connections suitable, protected, and safe to use in the mobile lab environment?
hazardStatement: Unsuitable or unprotected electrical equipment in a mobile lab environment creates shock and fire risks, particularly where flammables are present.
consequenceStatement: Electric shock, fire, or explosion from electrical fault in the mobile lab.
recommendation: Use only rated, protected electrical equipment suitable for the mobile lab environment; inspect connections before use; maintain records.
guidanceText: Check power supply arrangements, generator condition, and electrical connections for damage, IP rating, and suitability for the environment.

text: Are emergency exits, doors, and internal access routes unobstructed and usable at all times?
hazardStatement: Obstructed exits prevent rapid evacuation during a fire, spill, or other emergency in the confined mobile lab space.
consequenceStatement: Inability to evacuate quickly causing serious injury or fatality during an emergency.
recommendation: Keep all emergency exits, doors, and internal routes clear at all times; include in pre-operation checks.
guidanceText: Check that exit doors open freely from inside, are unobstructed, and that the internal route to exits is clear.

text: Is a suitable spill kit available, stocked, and readily accessible in the van?
hazardStatement: Absence of a spill kit in the mobile lab prevents prompt response to chemical spills, increasing exposure and contamination risk.
consequenceStatement: Chemical exposure and environmental contamination from uncontrolled spills in the mobile lab.
recommendation: Ensure a chemical spill kit matched to the substances carried is stocked and accessible inside the vehicle.
guidanceText: Locate and inspect the spill kit; verify contents are appropriate for the chemicals used and the kit is fully stocked.

text: Are eyewash and immediate flushing arrangements available where corrosives or injurious materials are handled, and are they maintained and unobstructed?
hazardStatement: Absence of eyewash or flushing equipment where corrosives are used can result in permanent eye or skin damage.
consequenceStatement: Permanent eye damage or severe chemical burns from delayed decontamination.
recommendation: Provide eyewash bottles or a portable eyewash station in the mobile lab; inspect and replace before expiry; keep unobstructed.
guidanceText: Locate eyewash equipment in the vehicle; check condition, expiry date, and accessibility.

text: Are fire extinguishers available, suitable for the hazards present, and unobstructed?
hazardStatement: Absence of appropriate fire extinguishers in the mobile lab leaves personnel unable to tackle incipient fires.
consequenceStatement: Fire escalation in the confined van environment causing serious injury or fatality.
recommendation: Fit the mobile lab with extinguishers appropriate for chemical and electrical fires; inspect and service regularly; keep accessible.
guidanceText: Check extinguisher type, service tag, charge indicator, and that it is accessible and unobstructed.

text: Are discarded chemicals, contaminated consumables, and hazardous waste stored in sealed, compatible, and properly labelled containers for transport and disposal?
hazardStatement: Improperly stored hazardous waste in the mobile lab creates exposure, spill, and contamination risks during transport.
