consequenceStatement: Chemical exposure, environmental contamination, and regulatory non-compliance from improper waste storage.
recommendation: Use sealed, compatible, labelled containers for all hazardous waste; arrange disposal through licensed contractors.
guidanceText: Inspect waste containers in the vehicle for correct labelling, sealing, and compatibility with contents.

text: Are transport and packaging arrangements suitable for samples, chemicals, and hazardous materials carried in the mobile lab?
hazardStatement: Unsuitable packaging for hazardous materials during transport can lead to spills, leaks, and chemical exposure.
consequenceStatement: Chemical spill, exposure, or regulatory non-compliance during transport of hazardous materials.
recommendation: Use UN-approved packaging where required; comply with transport regulations for hazardous materials; train drivers.
guidanceText: Check packaging used for chemical and sample transport; verify compliance with applicable transport regulations.

text: Do personnel know the response actions for spills, leaks, fire, exposure, vehicle breakdown, or power loss in the mobile lab?
hazardStatement: Personnel unaware of emergency response actions cannot respond effectively to incidents in the mobile lab.
consequenceStatement: Escalation of spill, fire, or exposure incident due to delayed or incorrect response.
recommendation: Train personnel in emergency response for all foreseeable mobile lab scenarios; post response actions inside the vehicle.
guidanceText: Ask personnel to describe response actions for a chemical spill or fire in the mobile lab; check posted emergency information.

text: Are appropriate gloves, eye/face protection, and other PPE available and worn as required for the work being performed?
hazardStatement: Absence or non-use of required PPE in the mobile lab exposes personnel to chemical, biological, or physical hazards.
consequenceStatement: Chemical exposure, eye injury, or other harm from inadequate PPE.
recommendation: Provide and enforce use of SDS-specified PPE; store PPE accessibly in the mobile lab; inspect for condition before use.
guidanceText: Check that appropriate PPE is available, in good condition, and being worn by personnel during laboratory work.

text: Is food, drink, and other non-laboratory use prohibited inside the laboratory area of the van?
hazardStatement: Eating or drinking in the laboratory area creates risk of inadvertent ingestion of hazardous substances.
consequenceStatement: Accidental ingestion of chemical or biological hazardous material.
recommendation: Post and enforce a prohibition on food and drink in the laboratory area; provide a designated eating area separate from the lab space.
guidanceText: Check for food or drink in the lab area; confirm prohibition signage is posted and enforced.

text: Are hazard information signs, emergency contacts, and required notices posted in or on the mobile laboratory where appropriate?
hazardStatement: Absent hazard signs and emergency contacts delay correct response and leave bystanders unaware of risks.
consequenceStatement: Delayed emergency response and uninformed bystander exposure to mobile lab hazards.
recommendation: Post hazard signs, emergency contacts, and required notices inside and on the exterior of the mobile lab where relevant.
guidanceText: Check interior and exterior of the mobile lab for required hazard signs, UN numbers where applicable, and emergency contact information.

text: High noise areas are identified and posted with warning signs
hazardStatement: Unidentified high noise areas expose workers to hearing damage.
consequenceStatement: Noise-induced hearing loss in exposed workers.
recommendation: Identify and sign all areas where noise exceeds 85 dB.
guidanceText: Check for noise warning signs near generators, compressors and workshops.

text: Noise levels are indicated on noisy equipment
hazardStatement: Unknown equipment noise levels prevent appropriate hearing protection decisions.
consequenceStatement: Hearing damage from exposure above safe levels.
recommendation: Label noise output on all equipment exceeding safe exposure levels.
guidanceText: Check equipment specification plates for noise output values.

text: Personnel working in elevated noise areas are aware of hazards and hearing protection rules
hazardStatement: Uninformed workers do not take protective action in noise areas.
consequenceStatement: Preventable noise-induced hearing loss.
recommendation: Conduct noise awareness training for workers in high noise areas.
guidanceText: Ask workers in noise areas to describe hearing protection requirements.

text: Hearing protection is provided to personnel when working in high noise areas or with equipment with elevated noise levels
hazardStatement: Absent hearing protection in noisy areas causes hearing damage.
consequenceStatement: Irreversible noise-induced hearing loss.
recommendation: Provide appropriate hearing protection and enforce its use.
guidanceText: Verify hearing protection is available and being worn in noise zones.

text: Ladders are in good condition (no missing or damaged rungs, no cracked or bent rails)
hazardStatement: Defective ladders collapse under load causing falls.
consequenceStatement: Falls from height resulting in serious injury or fatality.
recommendation: Remove and discard all damaged ladders from service.
guidanceText: Physically inspect every rung, rail and foot of all ladders.

text: Extension ladders with cable or deck hooks attached have no visible signs of fatigue
hazardStatement: Fatigued hooks fail under load causing the ladder to fall.
consequenceStatement: Fall from height causing serious injury.
recommendation: Inspect and replace hooks showing fatigue cracks or deformation.
guidanceText: Examine hook attachment points for cracks or deformation.

text: Ladders are equipped with non-slip feet
hazardStatement: Ladders without non-slip feet slide causing falls.
consequenceStatement: Falls from height resulting in serious injury.
recommendation: Replace worn or missing non-slip feet on all ladders.
guidanceText: Check rubber feet on all ladder bases for wear and security.

text: Wooden ladders are not painted (a clear finish may be used)
hazardStatement: Paint conceals cracks and defects in wooden ladder rungs.
consequenceStatement: Undetected defect causes rung failure and fall.
recommendation: Strip paint from wooden ladders and apply clear varnish only.
guidanceText: Check wooden ladders for paint and inspect for hidden defects.

text: Workers do not stand on the top two steps or rungs of a portable ladder
hazardStatement: Standing on the top rungs reduces stability and fall protection.
consequenceStatement: Falls from height causing serious injury or fatality.
recommendation: Brief workers on safe ladder use and enforce the rule actively.
guidanceText: Observe ladder use tasks and check workers do not exceed safe standing height.

text: Step ladders are not used as straight ladders
hazardStatement: Step ladders leaned against walls are unstable and unsafe.
consequenceStatement: Falls from height from ladder collapse.
recommendation: Provide appropriate straight ladders where vertical access is required.
guidanceText: Check that all step ladders are used only in their open A-frame position.

text: Extension ladders are equipped with locks that securely hold sections in the extended position
hazardStatement: Unlocked extension ladders collapse under load.
consequenceStatement: Fall from height causing serious injury or fatality.
recommendation: Replace ladders with defective or missing locking mechanisms.
guidanceText: Test locking mechanism on all extension ladders.

text: Portable ladders (other than step ladders) are set up at a 1:4 angle ratio and tied off
hazardStatement: Incorrect angle and unsecured ladders slip causing falls.
consequenceStatement: Falls from height resulting in serious injury.
recommendation: Train workers on correct ladder angle and tie-off procedures.
guidanceText: Observe ladder placement and verify 1:4 angle and top tie-off.

text: Extension ladders used to access a roof or platform extend three visible rungs (1 metre) above the roof or platform edge
hazardStatement: Ladder not extending beyond platform edge provides no handhold during transition.
consequenceStatement: Fall from height during transition on or off ladder.
recommendation: Extend ladders at least 1 metre above any access platform or roof.
guidanceText: Measure ladder extension above the access point during observation.

text: Scaffold is erected by competent personnel
hazardStatement: Incompetently erected scaffold collapses under load.
consequenceStatement: Collapse causing fatality or multiple serious injuries.
recommendation: Verify competency certificates for all scaffold erectors.
guidanceText: Request competency records for all scaffold erectors on site.

text: Scaffolding appears to be in good condition
hazardStatement: Damaged scaffold components can fail under load.
consequenceStatement: Scaffold collapse causing serious injury or fatality.
recommendation: Remove damaged components and arrange inspection by a competent person.
guidanceText: Visually inspect scaffold members for damage, rust and deformation.

text: Scaffolding is inspected daily by a competent person before use
hazardStatement: Uninspected scaffold may have overnight damage or displacement.
consequenceStatement: Fall from scaffold failure.
recommendation: Implement and enforce a daily scaffold inspection register.
guidanceText: Request daily inspection records and verify they are current.

text: Guard rails are in place on all scaffolding over three metres in height
hazardStatement: Absence of guard rails on elevated scaffold leads to falls.
consequenceStatement: Fall from height causing fatality or serious injury.
recommendation: Immediately install guardrails on all scaffolding above 3 metres.
guidanceText: Measure scaffold height and verify full guardrail installation.

text: Scaffold platform is at least 0.5 metres wide for light duty work and 1 metre wide for heavy duty work
hazardStatement: Narrow platforms increase the risk of workers overbalancing.
consequenceStatement: Falls from height due to insufficient working width.
recommendation: Widen platform to meet minimum width requirements for the task.
guidanceText: Measure platform width and compare to duty classification.

text: Planks and decks are laid tightly side by side to cover the full width of the platform
hazardStatement: Gaps between planks allow tools and feet to fall through.
consequenceStatement: Fall or struck-by injury from falling materials.
recommendation: Ensure planks are abutted and cover the full platform width.
guidanceText: Check that no gaps exist between scaffold planks.

text: Scaffold planks and platforms do not extend less than 150 mm or more than 300 mm beyond the bearers
hazardStatement: Incorrect plank overhang causes tipping or snagging.
consequenceStatement: Fall from height or struck-by injury.
recommendation: Adjust plank overhangs to within the 150–300 mm required range.
guidanceText: Measure overhang at both ends of scaffold planks.

text: Toe boards are installed on scaffolds over three metres in height
hazardStatement: Absent toe boards allow materials and tools to be kicked off the platform.
consequenceStatement: Struck-by injury to persons below from falling objects.
recommendation: Install toe boards of at least 150 mm height on all elevated platforms.
guidanceText: Check that toe boards are present and correctly fitted around the platform perimeter.

text: Rolling scaffolds have a working brake on all wheels
hazardStatement: Unbraked rolling scaffolds move unexpectedly causing falls.
consequenceStatement: Fall from height during uncontrolled scaffold movement.
recommendation: Test all wheel brakes before use and lock prior to mounting.
guidanceText: Test each wheel brake on rolling scaffolds during inspection.

text: Scaffold base plates are securely affixed to the floor or ground under at least two consecutive supports
hazardStatement: Unsecured base plates allow scaffold to shift under load.
consequenceStatement: Scaffold instability or collapse.
recommendation: Secure base plates and ensure sole boards are in place on soft ground.
guidanceText: Check base plate fixings at each scaffold standard.

text: Decks and planks are secured to prevent accidental movement in any direction
hazardStatement: Unsecured planks can shift or flip causing falls.
consequenceStatement: Fall from height due to plank displacement.
recommendation: Clip or tie all scaffold planks to prevent movement.
guidanceText: Attempt to move planks by hand to check they are secured.

text: Scaffolds over four metres in height must be vertically anchored
hazardStatement: Unanchored tall scaffolds are at risk of overturning.
consequenceStatement: Scaffold collapse causing fatality or multiple injuries.
recommendation: Install vertical anchors to building structure on all scaffolds over 4 metres.
guidanceText: Verify tie or anchor points on scaffolds exceeding 4 metres.

text: Fire extinguisher(s) are within nine metres of where hot work is performed
hazardStatement: Absent nearby extinguisher prevents immediate response to hot work fire.
consequenceStatement: Fire escalation from hot work ignition.
recommendation: Place a serviceable extinguisher within 9 metres of all hot work.
guidanceText: Measure distance from extinguisher to hot work location.

text: Compressed gas cylinders are in an upright position and properly secured
hazardStatement: Unsecured or horizontal cylinders become projectiles if valves are damaged.
consequenceStatement: Serious injury or fatality from cylinder impact or gas release.
recommendation: Chain all cylinders upright to a fixed point.
guidanceText: Check all cylinders are vertical, chained and valve caps fitted.

text: Acetylene bottles are in an upright position (all sizes must remain upright for a minimum of one hour prior to use if previously on their side)
hazardStatement: Using acetylene from a horizontal bottle releases unstable dissolved solvent.
consequenceStatement: Fire or explosion from unstable acetylene delivery.
recommendation: Ensure all acetylene bottles are stored and used only in the upright position.
guidanceText: Check acetylene cylinder orientation and ask about recent handling.

text: Flash curtains are used to protect others from arc flash including outdoor work
hazardStatement: Unscreened arc flash causes eye injury to bystanders.
consequenceStatement: Arc eye injury to persons in the vicinity of welding.
recommendation: Erect welding curtains around all arc welding areas.
guidanceText: Check for welding curtains in all arc welding locations.

text: Welding fumes are exhausted to the outside (via fume hood or portable exhaust unit)
hazardStatement: Welding fumes in enclosed spaces cause respiratory disease.
consequenceStatement: Chronic lung disease from repeated fume inhalation.
recommendation: Install or use LEV or portable exhaust units during all indoor hot work.
guidanceText: Confirm local exhaust ventilation is in operation during welding.

text: All contractors conducting hot work have acquired a valid hot work permit prior to conducting work
hazardStatement: Unpermitted hot work removes the safety barrier controls.
consequenceStatement: Fire or explosion from uncontrolled ignition source.
recommendation: Enforce hot work permit system for all contractors.
guidanceText: Request and review permits before any hot work inspection.

text: Hot work permits are posted for the duration of hot work
hazardStatement: Unposted permits prevent verification of controls at the work site.
consequenceStatement: Uninspected hot work without adequate safety controls.
recommendation: Require permits to be displayed at the hot work location at all times.
guidanceText: Look for permit displayed at the hot work area.

text: Combustible materials have been removed from the welding area
hazardStatement: Combustibles near hot work ignite from sparks and spatter.
consequenceStatement: Fire causing property damage, injury or fatality.
recommendation: Clear a minimum 10-metre radius of combustibles before hot work begins.
guidanceText: Inspect the area around the welding location for combustible materials.

text: Are lubricant storage and handling areas clearly identified and appropriately laid out?
hazardStatement: Poorly identified or laid-out lubricant areas increase risk of spills, contamination, and exposure.
consequenceStatement: Spill incidents, chemical exposure, and environmental contamination.
recommendation: Clearly mark lubricant storage areas; arrange to allow safe access and separation from incompatible materials.
guidanceText: Check that lubricant areas are labelled, logical in layout, and allow safe movement around containers.

text: Are lubricant containers, drums, and decanted containers properly labelled?
hazardStatement: Unlabelled containers lead to misuse, accidental mixing, and inability to apply correct emergency response.
consequenceStatement: Chemical exposure, fire, environmental contamination, and regulatory non-compliance.
recommendation: Label all containers with product name, hazard information, and date; prohibit use of unlabelled containers.
guidanceText: Check all containers and drums in storage and work areas for correct, legible labelling.

text: Are current Safety Data Sheets (SDS) available and accessible to personnel handling lubricants?
hazardStatement: Absence of SDS leaves personnel without critical hazard, PPE, and emergency response information.
consequenceStatement: Incorrect handling, inadequate PPE, and ineffective emergency response.
recommendation: Maintain current SDS at lubricant storage and use points; update when products change.
guidanceText: Verify SDS are present, accessible, and within 5-year revision cycle for all lubricants on-site.

text: Is ventilation adequate in lubricant storage, dispensing, and handling areas?
hazardStatement: Inadequate ventilation causes build-up of vapours and fumes that create health and fire risks.
consequenceStatement: Vapour exposure causing illness, or ignition leading to fire or explosion.
recommendation: Ensure natural or mechanical ventilation is adequate; check SDS for ventilation requirements.
guidanceText: Assess airflow in lubricant areas; check for odours indicating inadequate ventilation.

text: Are personnel aware of the lubricants they work with and trained in safe handling, storage, and emergency response?
hazardStatement: Untrained personnel handling lubricants may not recognise hazards or respond correctly to spills or exposure.
consequenceStatement: Chemical exposure, environmental contamination, and ineffective emergency response.
recommendation: Train all personnel who handle lubricants; cover hazards, SDS use, PPE, spill response, and emergency contacts.
guidanceText: Check training records; ask personnel about the hazards of lubricants they handle and spill response procedure.

text: Are warning signs posted at lubricant storage and handling areas, consistent with the hazards present?
hazardStatement: Absent warning signs leave uninformed personnel unaware of chemical hazards.
consequenceStatement: Accidental exposure or ignition due to unrecognised hazards.
recommendation: Post GHS hazard signs, prohibition signs, and PPE mandatory signs at lubricant storage and handling areas.
guidanceText: Inspect lubricant areas for appropriate hazard, prohibition, and PPE mandatory signage.

text: Are emergency response procedures available for lubricant spills, leaks, fire, and related incidents?
hazardStatement: Absence of documented emergency procedures leads to slow and incorrect response to spills or fires.
consequenceStatement: Injury, environmental contamination, and escalation of incidents due to delayed or incorrect response.
recommendation: Document and post spill, fire, and exposure response procedures at lubricant areas; train personnel.
guidanceText: Check that emergency procedures are posted and accessible at lubricant storage and handling areas.

text: Is emergency contact information posted and up to date?
hazardStatement: Outdated or absent emergency contacts delay response during a spill or chemical exposure incident.
consequenceStatement: Delayed emergency response and increased harm.
recommendation: Post current emergency contacts at lubricant areas; review and update at least annually.
guidanceText: Check posted emergency contact information for currency and completeness.

text: Is a current lubricant and hazardous material inventory maintained?
hazardStatement: Without an accurate inventory, personnel and emergency responders cannot identify all chemical hazards on site.
consequenceStatement: Unidentified hazards and incorrect emergency response.
recommendation: Maintain a current chemical inventory; update when products are added or removed; align with SDS register.
guidanceText: Request the chemical inventory and verify it reflects current lubricant stocks.

text: Do personnel know the location of and have access to up-to-date SDS for lubricants in their work area?
hazardStatement: Personnel unable to locate SDS cannot apply correct controls or respond correctly to exposure incidents.
consequenceStatement: Incorrect handling and inadequate emergency response to chemical exposure.
recommendation: Ensure SDS locations are communicated during induction; display SDS index at storage areas.
guidanceText: Ask a random worker to locate the SDS for a lubricant they handle; time the access.

text: Do personnel know the nearest location of emergency eyewash, emergency shower where required, spill kits, and fire extinguishers?
hazardStatement: Personnel unaware of emergency equipment locations cannot respond quickly to exposure or spill incidents.
consequenceStatement: Delayed emergency response causing greater injury or contamination.
recommendation: Include emergency equipment locations in induction; mark locations clearly; conduct periodic drills.
guidanceText: Ask personnel handling lubricants to identify the nearest eyewash, spill kit, and fire extinguisher.

text: Are lubricant containers and drums kept in good condition, with no leaks, corrosion, or damage?
hazardStatement: Leaking or corroded containers release lubricants creating slip, fire, and environmental hazards.
consequenceStatement: Slip injury, fire, and environmental contamination.
recommendation: Inspect containers and drums regularly; remove and replace damaged or leaking items immediately.
guidanceText: Inspect all containers and drums for dents, corrosion, leaks, or damaged seals.

text: Are lubricant liquids kept in sealed or closed containers when not in use?
hazardStatement: Open containers allow evaporation of vapours, increasing fire risk and personnel exposure.
consequenceStatement: Fire or explosion from accumulated vapours, and health effects from vapour inhalation.
recommendation: Enforce a closed-container policy for lubricants; seal containers immediately after use.
guidanceText: Check that lubricant containers are sealed or capped when not actively in use.

text: Is secondary containment provided for lubricant drums, containers, or liquids where required?
hazardStatement: Absence of secondary containment allows lubricant spills to reach drains and cause environmental contamination.
consequenceStatement: Environmental contamination, regulatory non-compliance, and clean-up costs.
recommendation: Provide bunded areas or drip trays for all bulk lubricant storage; check capacity meets regulatory requirements.
guidanceText: Check that bunds or drip trays are in place, intact, and have adequate capacity for the stored volume.

text: Are lubricant transfer, dispensing, and decanting activities carried out in a way that prevents spills and contamination?
hazardStatement: Uncontrolled transfer and decanting activities cause spills that create slip, fire, and environmental hazards.
consequenceStatement: Slip injury, fire, and environmental contamination from uncontrolled spills.
recommendation: Use appropriate transfer equipment; conduct transfers over containment; train personnel in spill prevention.
guidanceText: Observe or review lubricant transfer procedures; check for evidence of past spills or residue.

text: Are spill kits, absorbents, and cleanup materials available where lubricants are stored or handled?
hazardStatement: Absence of spill response materials allows spills to spread, increasing contamination and slip hazard.
consequenceStatement: Environmental contamination and slip injury from uncontrolled lubricant spills.
recommendation: Place spill kits at lubricant storage and handling areas; inspect and replenish regularly; train personnel in use.
guidanceText: Verify spill kits are present, correctly stocked, and accessible at all lubricant areas.

text: Are incompatible substances stored separately from lubricants where mixing could create risk?
hazardStatement: Co-storage of incompatible substances with lubricants can cause fire, toxic release, or dangerous reactions.
consequenceStatement: Fire, explosion, or toxic release from incompatible chemical mixing.
recommendation: Use a chemical segregation plan based on SDS compatibility data; label storage areas accordingly.
guidanceText: Check lubricant storage areas for co-stored chemicals; verify incompatible materials are physically separated.

text: Are used-oil containers and tanks, where applicable, clearly labelled and kept in good condition?
hazardStatement: Unlabelled or damaged used-oil containers risk misidentification, leaks, and environmental contamination.
consequenceStatement: Environmental contamination, regulatory non-compliance, and potential fire hazard.
recommendation: Label all used-oil containers with 'Used Oil' and hazard information; inspect for leaks or damage regularly.
guidanceText: Inspect used-oil storage containers and tanks for labelling, condition, and signs of leakage.

text: Where used oil is generated, is it stored in suitable containers or tanks and managed to prevent leaks or environmental contamination?
hazardStatement: Improperly stored used oil causes environmental contamination and creates fire and slip hazards.
consequenceStatement: Environmental contamination, regulatory non-compliance, and potential fire.
recommendation: Store used oil in sealed, labelled, secondary-contained tanks or drums; arrange collection with licensed contractor.
guidanceText: Check used-oil storage for containment, labelling, condition, and evidence of disposal arrangements.

text: Are ladders inspected before use and free from visible damage or defects?
hazardStatement: Damaged ladders can fail during use, causing falls from height.
consequenceStatement: Serious injury or fatality from fall due to ladder failure.
recommendation: Inspect ladders before each use; remove defective ladders from service and label 'Do Not Use'.
guidanceText: Check rungs, stiles, feet, and locking mechanisms for cracks, bending, corrosion, or missing components.

text: Are ladders used only for the purpose they were designed for and on stable, level surfaces?
hazardStatement: Using ladders on unstable surfaces or for unintended purposes increases the risk of falls.
consequenceStatement: Serious injury or fatality from fall.
recommendation: Train personnel on correct ladder use; ensure ladders are placed on firm, level ground only.
guidanceText: Observe ladder use; check footing and whether the ladder type is appropriate for the task.

text: Are step ladders used correctly and not used as straight ladders?
hazardStatement: Misusing step ladders as straight ladders reduces stability and increases fall risk.
consequenceStatement: Fall injury due to ladder instability.
recommendation: Train personnel on correct step ladder use; ensure spreaders are fully open and locked during use.
guidanceText: Observe step ladder use; check that spreaders are open and the ladder is used as designed.

text: Are leaning ladders set at a safe angle and secured where they could slip or be displaced?
hazardStatement: Ladders set at incorrect angles or not secured can slip, causing falls from height.
consequenceStatement: Serious injury or fatality from fall due to ladder slipping.
recommendation: Set leaning ladders at a 75° angle (1 out for every 4 up); tie or hold the ladder at the top and foot.
guidanceText: Check leaning ladder angle and whether the base and top are secured against slipping.

text: When used for access to a higher level, do ladders extend sufficiently above the landing point or provide equivalent handhold protection?
hazardStatement: Ladders that do not extend above the landing point leave no handhold when stepping on or off, increasing fall risk.
consequenceStatement: Fall injury when stepping on or off the ladder at height.
recommendation: Ensure ladders extend at least 1 metre above the landing point or provide an equivalent secure handhold.
guidanceText: Check ladder height relative to the landing point; measure or estimate the extension above the platform.

text: Is scaffolding erected, altered, and dismantled by competent personnel?
hazardStatement: Scaffolding erected by incompetent personnel may be structurally unsafe, risking collapse.
consequenceStatement: Scaffold collapse causing serious injury or fatality.
recommendation: Ensure only trained and competent scaffolders erect, alter, and dismantle scaffolding; check competency records.
guidanceText: Request competency evidence for scaffolders; check that work is being supervised by a competent person.

text: Is scaffolding inspected before use and after any event that could affect its integrity?
hazardStatement: Uninspected scaffolding may have defects that cause collapse during use.
consequenceStatement: Scaffold failure leading to serious injury or fatality.
recommendation: Inspect scaffolding before use, weekly, and after adverse weather or impact; maintain written inspection records.
guidanceText: Check scaffold inspection records; look for a current tag or handover certificate on the scaffold.

text: Is scaffolding in sound condition, properly founded, and not overloaded?
hazardStatement: Poorly founded or overloaded scaffolding can collapse under load.
consequenceStatement: Scaffold collapse causing serious injury or fatality.
recommendation: Check base plates, sole boards, and foundations before use; enforce load limits and prevent overloading.
guidanceText: Inspect scaffold base for stability; check that base plates and sole boards are in place; look for overloading.

text: Are platforms fully decked or otherwise suitably boarded, with components secured against movement?
hazardStatement: Gaps in scaffold platforms or unsecured boards can cause falls through or tripping.
consequenceStatement: Fall injury from gap in platform or tripping on unsecured board.
recommendation: Ensure platforms are fully boarded with no gaps wider than 25mm; secure boards against movement and wind.
guidanceText: Inspect platform decking for gaps, missing boards, and unsecured or slippery boards.

text: Are required guardrails, toe boards, access arrangements, ties, and wheel brakes provided where applicable?
hazardStatement: Missing guardrails, toe boards, or ties on scaffolding allows falls of persons or materials.
consequenceStatement: Fall of person or materials causing serious injury or fatality.
recommendation: Check that guardrails, mid-rails, toe boards, ties, and brakes are in place and intact before use.
guidanceText: Walk around the scaffold and check for guardrails, toe boards, ties, and brakes on mobile scaffold wheels.

text: Is hot work controlled through a valid permit or equivalent authorisation where required?
hazardStatement: Hot work without a permit increases the risk of uncontrolled fire in the workplace.
consequenceStatement: Fire, explosion, injury, and property damage due to uncontrolled hot work.
recommendation: Implement a hot work permit system; ensure permits are issued, completed, and closed out for all hot work.
guidanceText: Request the hot work permit for any active hot work; check it is valid, complete, and signed.

text: Have combustible materials been removed or protected before hot work starts?
hazardStatement: Combustible materials near hot work can be ignited by sparks or heat, causing fire.
consequenceStatement: Fire leading to property damage, injury, or fatality.
recommendation: Clear a minimum 11-metre radius of combustible materials before hot work; use fire blankets where removal is not possible.
guidanceText: Inspect the hot work area for combustible materials, flammable liquids, and vapours before work begins.

text: Are suitable fire extinguishers immediately available and is a fire watch provided where required?
hazardStatement: Absence of fire extinguishers or a fire watch at hot work allows fires to go undetected or uncontrolled.
consequenceStatement: Uncontrolled fire leading to serious property damage, injury, or fatality.
recommendation: Position appropriate extinguishers at the hot work location; assign a fire watch during and after hot work as required.
guidanceText: Confirm extinguisher type, charge, and accessibility; check that a fire watch is assigned during and after hot work.

text: Are compressed gas cylinders kept upright, secured, and handled or stored safely?
hazardStatement: Unsecured or incorrectly handled cylinders can fall, causing physical injury or catastrophic gas release.
consequenceStatement: Physical injury from falling cylinder, or fire and explosion from uncontrolled gas release.
recommendation: Store cylinders upright and chained or bracketed; cap when not in use; transport with appropriate trolleys.
guidanceText: Check that cylinders are secured upright with chains or brackets, capped when not in use, and stored away from heat.

text: Are welding fumes effectively controlled by local exhaust, extraction, or other suitable controls?
hazardStatement: Welding fumes cause serious respiratory disease and are classified as carcinogenic.
consequenceStatement: Respiratory illness and long-term occupational disease from fume exposure.
recommendation: Use local exhaust ventilation (LEV) at the source; ensure natural ventilation is supplemented in enclosed areas; use appropriate respiratory protection.
guidanceText: Check that LEV is in place and operational during welding; assess adequacy of ventilation in the work area.

text: Are nearby workers protected from sparks, slag, and arc flash by screens, curtains, or other suitable barriers?
hazardStatement: Sparks, slag, and arc flash from hot work can injure nearby workers and ignite combustibles.
consequenceStatement: Eye injury, burns, and fire from uncontrolled sparks or arc flash.
recommendation: Use welding screens or fire blankets to contain sparks and protect nearby workers; enforce exclusion zones.
guidanceText: Check that screens or curtains are in place around hot work; confirm nearby personnel are protected.

text: Has a task-specific manual handling risk assessment or HIRA been completed where required?
hazardStatement: Without a risk assessment, manual handling hazards may be unidentified and controls absent.
consequenceStatement: Musculoskeletal injury due to uncontrolled manual handling hazards.
recommendation: Complete a manual handling risk assessment for significant handling tasks; review when tasks or conditions change.
guidanceText: Request and review manual handling risk assessment for the tasks observed.

text: Are hazardous manual handling tasks avoided or reduced where reasonably practicable?
hazardStatement: Failure to eliminate or reduce hazardous manual handling increases musculoskeletal injury risk.
consequenceStatement: Musculoskeletal disorders and lost-time injury from avoidable manual handling.
recommendation: Apply the hierarchy of control to manual handling; eliminate, substitute, or mechanise tasks before relying on technique training.
guidanceText: Identify manual handling tasks; check whether handling aids, redesigned tasks, or mechanisation has been considered.

text: Are frequently used and heavier items stored between knee and shoulder height where practicable?
hazardStatement: Items stored outside the power zone require awkward postures that increase injury risk.
consequenceStatement: Back and shoulder injury from lifting at low or overhead heights.
recommendation: Reorganise storage to place heavy and frequently handled items in the knee-to-shoulder zone.
guidanceText: Check storage locations for heavy and frequently used items relative to working height.

text: Are suitable manual handling aids, such as trolleys, carts, or safe access steps, available and used where needed?
hazardStatement: Absence of handling aids forces workers to manually carry loads that create musculoskeletal risk.
consequenceStatement: Musculoskeletal injury from manually carrying loads that could be mechanised.
recommendation: Provide and maintain trolleys, carts, sack trucks, and other aids; train personnel to use them.
guidanceText: Check that handling aids are available, in good condition, and being used for appropriate tasks.

text: Are loads of manageable size, shape, and weight for the people handling them?
hazardStatement: Loads that are too heavy or bulky create excessive musculoskeletal strain.
consequenceStatement: Acute or cumulative musculoskeletal injury from overloaded handling.
recommendation: Assess load weights and sizes; split loads, use team lifts, or provide mechanical aids where limits are exceeded.
guidanceText: Observe handling tasks; check whether load weights and dimensions are within safe handling limits.

text: Are repetitive handling, awkward postures, and excessive reach distances minimised?
hazardStatement: Repetitive handling and sustained awkward postures are a primary cause of musculoskeletal disorders.
consequenceStatement: Work-related musculoskeletal disorders and lost-time injury.
recommendation: Redesign tasks, introduce job rotation, and modify workstations to reduce repetition and awkward postures.
guidanceText: Observe work tasks for repetitive movements, awkward bending, twisting, or excessive reaching.

text: Do personnel understand and apply safe lifting and handling techniques?
hazardStatement: Incorrect lifting technique significantly increases the risk of musculoskeletal injury.
consequenceStatement: Acute or cumulative back and musculoskeletal injury.
recommendation: Provide manual handling training; include correct technique, load limits, and use of aids; verify understanding.
guidanceText: Observe lifting activities; ask personnel to describe safe lifting technique; check training records.

text: Are lubricant cartons stored, where practicable, between knee and shoulder height to reduce bending and overreaching?
hazardStatement: Lubricant cartons stored at floor or overhead levels require awkward postures that increase injury risk.
consequenceStatement: Back and shoulder injury from handling cartons at awkward heights.
recommendation: Arrange lubricant carton storage in the knee-to-shoulder zone; reserve floor and upper shelves for lighter or infrequently used items.
guidanceText: Check storage rack heights for lubricant carton storage; identify heavy items stored at floor or overhead level.

text: Are heavier lubricant cartons of manageable size and weight for the persons handling them?
hazardStatement: Lubricant cartons that are too heavy or bulky create excessive musculoskeletal strain during handling.
consequenceStatement: Back and shoulder injury from handling overweight lubricant cartons.
recommendation: Assess carton weights; consider smaller pack sizes or team handling for heavy cartons; provide trolleys or carts.
guidanceText: Check the weight of lubricant cartons being handled; confirm they are within safe limits for individual handling.

text: Are trolleys, carts, or other suitable aids available and used for moving lubricant cartons and packaged stock?
hazardStatement: Manual carrying of lubricant cartons over distances increases injury risk from cumulative loading.
consequenceStatement: Musculoskeletal injury from repetitive carrying of lubricant cartons.
recommendation: Provide trolleys and carts for moving lubricant cartons; train personnel to use them; maintain in good condition.
guidanceText: Check that trolleys are available and in use for moving lubricant cartons and packaged stock.

text: Are full lubricant drums moved using suitable drum-handling equipment rather than manual lifting?
hazardStatement: Full drums are extremely heavy and manually lifting or rolling them causes serious musculoskeletal or crush injury.
consequenceStatement: Serious back injury or crush injury from manual drum handling.
recommendation: Provide drum cradles, trolleys, or forklifts for drum movement; prohibit manual lifting of full drums.
guidanceText: Observe drum handling activities; check that mechanical equipment is used and personnel are not manually lifting full drums.

text: Are workers prevented from attempting to lift or raise full drums alone?
hazardStatement: Solo manual lifting of full drums routinely causes serious musculoskeletal injury.
consequenceStatement: Serious back or shoulder injury from attempting to lift a full drum alone.
recommendation: Prohibit solo manual lifting of full drums; enforce mechanical handling; display prohibition signage.
guidanceText: Confirm that all drum movement involves mechanical equipment or multiple persons; check for prohibition signage.

text: Are stacked drums handled using appropriate mechanical equipment rather than manual lifting from stacks?
hazardStatement: Manually handling drums from stacks is extremely hazardous due to weight and instability.
consequenceStatement: Crush or impact injury from drum falling from a stack.
recommendation: Use forklifts or drum-stacking equipment for all stacked drum handling; train personnel and enforce the procedure.
guidanceText: Observe drum stacking and de-stacking activities; confirm mechanical equipment is in use.

text: Are drum storage areas arranged to allow safe access, turning, and use of drum-handling equipment without obstruction?
hazardStatement: Congested drum storage areas prevent safe use of drum-handling equipment, forcing unsafe manual handling.
consequenceStatement: Musculoskeletal or crush injury from working in a congested drum storage area.
recommendation: Maintain adequate aisle widths for drum-handling equipment; keep drum areas clear of obstructions.
guidanceText: Check drum storage areas for aisle width, access for equipment, and absence of obstructions.

text: Are lubricant cartons and drums stacked and stored so they remain stable and secure against sliding, rolling, or collapse?
hazardStatement: Unstable stacking of cartons or drums creates falling-load hazards that can cause crush injury.
consequenceStatement: Crush or impact injury from falling cartons or drums.
recommendation: Observe stacking height limits; use drum cradles or chocks; ensure cartons are stacked evenly and within safe height limits.
guidanceText: Inspect stacked cartons and drums for stability, height, and use of chocks or cradles to prevent rolling.

text: Are dispensing or decanting arrangements provided to minimise unnecessary manual handling of full drums?
hazardStatement: Without dispensing arrangements, workers manually tilt or lift full drums to decant, causing injury risk.
consequenceStatement: Musculoskeletal injury from manual tilting or lifting of full drums during dispensing.
recommendation: Provide drum pumps, taps, or dispensing cradles to allow product to be drawn without manual drum lifting.
guidanceText: Check that drum pumps or dispensing equipment are available and in use at lubricant dispensing points.

text: Are adequate rest breaks taken during repetitive tasks or tasks requiring sustained postures?
hazardStatement: Continuous repetitive work without rest increases cumulative musculoskeletal loading and injury risk.
consequenceStatement: Cumulative musculoskeletal disorders from fatigue and overuse.
recommendation: Implement scheduled micro-breaks during repetitive tasks; introduce job rotation to vary posture and muscle use.
guidanceText: Ask supervisors and workers whether rest breaks are taken during repetitive tasks; review work schedules.

text: Is waste segregated into hazardous and non-hazardous waste streams?
hazardStatement: Failure to segregate hazardous waste contaminates non-hazardous streams and creates regulatory and environmental risk.
consequenceStatement: Environmental contamination, regulatory non-compliance, and increased disposal costs.
recommendation: Provide clearly labelled separate containers for hazardous and non-hazardous waste; train staff on correct segregation.
guidanceText: Check waste containers in office areas for correct segregation labelling and appropriate use.

text: Are hazardous and non-hazardous waste containers clearly labelled and used correctly?
hazardStatement: Unlabelled or incorrectly used waste containers lead to mixing of waste streams and environmental non-compliance.
consequenceStatement: Environmental contamination and regulatory non-compliance from incorrect waste segregation.
recommendation: Label all waste containers with waste type and hazard information; include in staff induction and training.
guidanceText: Inspect waste containers for correct labelling; check that contents match the labelled waste stream.

text: Are bins provided in suitable locations and emptied regularly?
hazardStatement: Insufficient or infrequently emptied bins lead to overflow and unhygienic conditions.
consequenceStatement: Unhygienic conditions, pest attraction, and staff complaints.
recommendation: Provide bins at convenient locations throughout office areas; include in daily cleaning and emptying rounds.
guidanceText: Check bin locations, capacity, and whether they are being emptied regularly.

text: Are waste containers not overflowing and emptied before overfilling occurs?
hazardStatement: Overflowing waste containers create unhygienic conditions and increase risk of spills and contamination.
consequenceStatement: Unhygienic conditions, pest attraction, and potential environmental contamination.
recommendation: Establish a regular waste collection schedule; supervise high-use areas to prevent overflow.
guidanceText: Inspect waste containers throughout the office; check that none are overflowing or excessively full.

text: Where contractor work has been completed, is the area left clean and free from waste, debris, spills, and contamination?
hazardStatement: Waste, debris, and contamination left by contractors creates environmental and health and safety risks.
consequenceStatement: Environmental contamination, slip hazards, and non-compliance with contractor management obligations.
recommendation: Include clean-up requirements in contractor scope of work; inspect areas before sign-off and withhold sign-off until complete.
guidanceText: Inspect areas recently vacated by contractors for waste, spills, surplus materials, or debris.

text: Is waste segregated into hazardous and non-hazardous waste streams?
hazardStatement: Unsegregated waste in the depot mixes hazardous and non-hazardous streams, creating contamination and disposal compliance failures.
consequenceStatement: Environmental contamination, regulatory non-compliance, and increased disposal costs.
recommendation: Provide separate, labelled containers for each waste stream; train all depot personnel on correct segregation.
guidanceText: Check waste segregation arrangements in the depot; verify containers are correctly labelled and used.

text: Are hazardous and non-hazardous waste containers clearly labelled and used correctly?
hazardStatement: Unlabelled or misused waste containers lead to cross-contamination and environmental non-compliance.
consequenceStatement: Environmental contamination and regulatory non-compliance from incorrect waste handling.
recommendation: Label all depot waste containers with waste type and hazard information; include in depot induction training.
guidanceText: Inspect waste containers in the depot for correct labelling; check contents match the labelled stream.

text: Are waste bins, skips, drums, and storage containers suitable, labelled, and in good condition?
hazardStatement: Damaged or unlabelled containers allow waste to leak or be mishandled, creating environmental and health risks.
consequenceStatement: Environmental contamination and health risks from damaged or misidentified waste containers.
recommendation: Inspect all depot waste containers regularly; replace damaged or unlabelled containers promptly.
guidanceText: Check bins, skips, and drums for damage, correct labelling, and suitability for their waste stream.

text: Are spill kits available, adequately stocked, and located where spills could occur?
hazardStatement: Absent or inadequate spill kits allow spills to spread, increasing environmental contamination and regulatory risk.
consequenceStatement: Environmental contamination and regulatory non-compliance from uncontrolled spills.
recommendation: Position spill kits at all fuel, oil, and chemical handling and storage areas; inspect and replenish regularly.
guidanceText: Verify spill kits are present, correctly stocked, and positioned close to areas where spills could occur.

text: Has an Environmental Aspect and Impact Assessment been conducted for depot operations, and is it current?
hazardStatement: Without a current Environmental Aspect and Impact Assessment, significant environmental risks may be unidentified and uncontrolled.
consequenceStatement: Uncontrolled environmental impacts leading to contamination, regulatory non-compliance, and reputational damage.
recommendation: Complete and review an Environmental Aspect and Impact Assessment for depot operations; update when activities, chemicals, or processes change.
guidanceText: Request and review the Environmental Aspect and Impact Assessment; check dates, approval, and coverage of depot activities.

text: Are personnel aware of spill response procedures and how to use spill control materials?
hazardStatement: Personnel unaware of spill response procedures cannot contain spills effectively, increasing environmental harm.
consequenceStatement: Environmental contamination from inadequate or delayed spill response.
recommendation: Train all depot personnel in spill response; conduct periodic drills; post spill response procedures at storage areas.
guidanceText: Ask depot personnel to describe spill response procedure and locate the spill kit; check training records.

text: Are oils, lubricants, fuels, and chemicals stored in a manner that prevents leaks and environmental contamination?
hazardStatement: Improperly stored liquids leak or spill, causing soil and groundwater contamination.
consequenceStatement: Environmental contamination, regulatory non-compliance, and significant remediation costs.
recommendation: Store all liquids in compatible, sealed containers within secondary containment; inspect storage areas regularly.
guidanceText: Inspect liquid storage areas for secondary containment, container condition, and evidence of leaks or staining.

text: Is secondary containment or bunding provided where required for drums, tanks, or liquid storage?
hazardStatement: Absence of secondary containment allows spills and leaks to reach drains, causing environmental contamination.
consequenceStatement: Environmental contamination and significant regulatory penalties.
recommendation: Install bunds or drip trays at all bulk liquid storage points; check capacity meets regulatory requirements.
guidanceText: Check that bunds or drip trays are in place, intact, and of adequate capacity for stored volumes.

text: Are waste oil, used filters, oily rags, absorbents, and contaminated materials collected, stored, and disposed of appropriately?
hazardStatement: Improper storage or disposal of oily waste contaminates soil, water, and drains.
consequenceStatement: Environmental contamination, regulatory non-compliance, and disposal liability.
recommendation: Collect waste oil and contaminated materials in labelled, sealed containers; arrange disposal through licensed contractors.
guidanceText: Inspect waste oil storage; check that containers are sealed, labelled, and disposal records are available.

text: Are chemical and lubricant containers kept closed, sealed, and in good condition when not in use?
hazardStatement: Open or damaged containers allow product to evaporate or leak, creating environmental and fire hazards.
consequenceStatement: Environmental contamination, fire risk, and product loss from open or damaged containers.
recommendation: Enforce a closed-container policy; inspect containers regularly; replace damaged containers immediately.
guidanceText: Check that containers are sealed or capped when not in active use; look for damaged, open, or leaking containers.

text: Are loading, unloading, transfer, and dispensing activities controlled to minimise spills and releases?
hazardStatement: Uncontrolled transfer and dispensing activities cause spills that contaminate the environment.
consequenceStatement: Environmental contamination and regulatory non-compliance from spills during liquid handling.
recommendation: Conduct all transfers over secondary containment; use appropriate equipment; train personnel in spill prevention.
guidanceText: Observe or review transfer and dispensing procedures; check for evidence of past spills or contamination.

text: Are incompatible wastes or substances stored separately to prevent dangerous reactions or contamination?
hazardStatement: Mixing incompatible wastes or substances can cause fire, explosion, or release of toxic gases.
consequenceStatement: Fire, explosion, toxic release, or environmental contamination from incompatible waste or substance mixing.
recommendation: Use a chemical and waste segregation plan; label storage areas; train depot personnel on incompatibility.
guidanceText: Check storage areas for correct segregation of incompatible wastes and substances.

text: Are waste containers, skips, drums, and storage areas not overflowing and managed to prevent pollution?
hazardStatement: Overflowing waste containers allow waste to escape to the environment, causing contamination.
consequenceStatement: Environmental contamination and regulatory non-compliance from overflowing waste storage.
recommendation: Establish a regular waste collection schedule; monitor and empty containers before they reach capacity.
guidanceText: Inspect all waste containers, skips, and drums in the depot for overflow or excessive fill levels.

text: After contractor work, is the area cleared of waste, debris, spills, surplus materials, and contamination?
hazardStatement: Waste and contamination left by contractors creates ongoing environmental and safety risk in the depot.
consequenceStatement: Environmental contamination, slip hazards, and contractor management non-compliance.
recommendation: Include clean-up obligations in contractor scopes; inspect and sign off only when area is clean and clear.
guidanceText: Inspect recently vacated contractor work areas for waste, spills, surplus materials, or debris.

text: Are required environmental approvals, permits, or clearance conditions in place and complied with where applicable?
hazardStatement: Operating without required environmental approvals or breaching permit conditions creates significant legal and environmental liability.
consequenceStatement: Regulatory non-compliance, facility closure, and reputational damage.
recommendation: Maintain a register of all environmental permits and approvals; monitor compliance conditions; renew before expiry.
guidanceText: Check environmental permits and approvals for currency and compliance with conditions.

text: Has environmental close-out been verified before the contractor work area is handed back for normal operations?
hazardStatement: Handback without environmental close-out verification risks ongoing contamination and regulatory liability.
consequenceStatement: Environmental contamination and regulatory non-compliance from incomplete contractor close-out.
recommendation: Conduct a formal environmental close-out inspection before handing back contractor work areas; document and sign off.
guidanceText: Check close-out inspection records; confirm the area has been inspected and cleared before handback.
